theme: accepting a charismatic envoy's promise of ALLIANCE AND REINFORCEMENT that may be a bluff or a trap
problem: You are a Spartan harmost holding the coastal fortress of Eion after Brasidas' death, facing Athenian counterstrikes. A charismatic envoy from the fickle Thracian king Seuthes arrives, promising 2,000 cavalry and peltasts to jointly retake Amphipolis—but demands you first admit his ‘honor guard’ of 300 men inside your walls to ‘prepare supply caches.’ The envoy’s charm is magnetic, his promises vivid. If genuine, this alliance could reverse your losses; if a ruse, you lose the fortress before the main force even moves. Anticipating Seuthes’ true intent, the Athenian reaction, your allies’ morale, and how the ephors will judge you is everything.
facets: The envoy’s charisma and the lure of decisive reinforcement create emotional momentum that collides with the rational suspicion of a trap, mirroring the fear-honor-interest triad.
The broader cascade: rejecting the offer may drive Seuthes into Athens’ arms, while accepting may trigger an immediate Athenian assault or encourage other Thracian tribes to test your weakness.
Your own troops and remaining allies are watching; a refusal could signal timidity and unravel the fragile Spartan-led coalition in the region, while a miscalculation could annihilate your command.

angles.directive: Seize the envoy and his honor guard the moment they enter, disarming them and holding them as hostages. Demand Seuthes send the promised host unescorted to prove his sincerity, threatening execution otherwise. Anticipate whether this will force Seuthes to drop the bluff or provoke an immediate Thracian assault on your weakened walls.

Praise the envoy’s offer effusively but insist on a prior token of good faith—a highborn hostage from Seuthes’ own household—before any guard enters. Meanwhile, secretly send messengers to recall scattered patrols and prepare a fallback to Amphipolis’s inland fortifications, buying time without outright refusal.

Welcome the honor guard publicly as honored allies, quarter them in a segregated wing of the fortress where they cannot seize the gates, and assign them visible but non-critical duties. Spread word to the Athenians that Seuthes has committed forces, bluffing a full alliance to deter an enemy attack while you test the Thracians’ behavior for signs of treachery.

Accept the offer but instead of letting the guard in, announce that you will march your entire garrison out to meet Seuthes’ main body in the field for an immediate joint strike on Amphipolis, turning the situation from a fortress defense into a mobile campaign. If Seuthes hesitates, you expose his bluff and threaten his own territory under the guise of alliance, reframing trust into a test of his commitment.

threads: I have the envoy and his 300 seized as they pass the gate, stripped of weapons, and bound in the courtyard. My herald then delivers an ultimatum: Seuthes must send the 2,000 cavalry and peltasts without escort to the plain outside Eion, or his men die. The bet is that Seuthes, valuing his picked honor guard, will either comply and prove his sincerity, or refuse and unmask his treachery—either way, the Athenians lose their Thracian synchronization for the assault they were surely planning.

I will receive the Thracian envoy with public honors, then demand his king’s own nephew as a hostage lodged in my inner keep before any gate opens—a test of Seuthes’ sincerity that costs me nothing if refused. Simultaneously, I dispatch runners to recall foraging parties and reinforce the inland redoubt at Amphipolis, signaling to Athens that I am consolidating, not panicking. The entire maneuver bets that Seuthes, if genuine, values the alliance enough to pay the blood-price, while his refusal exposes the ruse without a single Spartan life lost and frames him as the oath-breaker before my allies.

I will publicly receive the 300 as honored allies, house them in the isolated eastern granary—far from the gates—and assign them to man the outer parapet under joint Spartan-Thracian watch, a visible but tactically sterile duty. Simultaneously, I seed a false report through a released Athenian scout that Seuthes has irrevocably committed, aiming to paralyze the enemy advance with doubt. The wager is that within days the Thracians’ constrained position will force revealing behavior—clandestine messengers or probing toward the sally port—exposing treason before they can coordinate any betrayal.

I inform the envoy that I accept the alliance and will immediately lead my entire garrison out to link up with Seuthes’ main force for a joint assault on Amphipolis, requiring his 300 to march with us as guides. The move forces an immediate binary: if he refuses or hesitates, his treachery is exposed before my wavering allies, solidifying their trust in my command. I then publicly pivot the column toward Seuthes’ own territory in a ‘show of allied solidarity,’ compelling him to either commit his full cavalry or see his borderlands ravaged, which fractures any Athenian hope of a Thracian proxy war.

judge.note: Strategies 3&4 assume Thracian behavior reveals itself quickly; unrealistic timescale.

judge6.note: Strategy 4 risks catastrophic overcommitment; hostage logic (2) assumes Seuthes values nephew equally.

theme: an able general operating FAR FROM HOME, with discretion to liberate or coerce cities beyond his orders' intent
problem: An Athenian strategos off the Chalcidice, with orders to hold the sea-lanes and avoid pitched battle, receives a clandestine overture from the democrats of Torone: they will open the gates if he brings his marines in force. But Brasidas is three days’ march away, his own trierarchs are restive, and the assembly’s mood is unforgiving—the wrong move could lose a fleet or a career.
facets: The democratic promise may be a genuine uprising or an elaborate Spartan ruse; the faction’s strength inside the walls is unknown.
Brasidas has a reputation for lightning counter-strokes; if he learns of the movement, he can cover the distance in under two days.
Athens adores a distant success but devours a failed commander—victory without authorisation brings triumph, defeat brings trial and exile.

angles.directive: Storm Torone with the entire marine force now, trusting speed and naval shock to seize the city and present Brasidas with a fortified fait accompli that dares him to attack.

Promise the democrats support, then conspicuously withdraw the fleet toward Sane, luring the Spartan garrison out to pursue; once Torone is stripped, double back by night and seize the unguarded harbour.

Bypass the democrats entirely and open secret talks with the oligarchic council, offering Athenian protection, trade concessions, and local autonomy if they themselves expel the Spartan harmost—making Torone a willing ally without a fight.

Invite all Chalcidic cities, regardless of faction, to a sacred Amphictyonic congress under Apollo’s patronage, recasting Athens not as imperial master but as founder of a new common peace that diplomatically isolates Brasidas and reframes the entire campaign.

threads: The strategos lands the full marine complement before dawn, breaches the gate the moment the faction signals, and floods the streets with armoured hoplites to seize the acropolis and the harbour before the city can organise resistance. Betting that the sheer violence of a nocturnal assault from within will paralyse any embedded Spartan sympathisers long enough to bar the gates against a returning Brasidas. When he arrives to find the walls manned by Athenian marines and the fleet offshore, he must either break his own doctrine in a costly assault or concede the city.

I signal the democrats that the fleet is coming, then conspicuously withdraw towards Sane, making our retreat unmistakable to the Spartan garrison. The move bets that the garrison commander, anxious to crush a democratic plot and seeing easy prey, will lead his main force out in pursuit along the shore. Once the city is emptied, we double back by night, land at the abandoned harbour, and lock the gates behind us before Brasidas knows the garrison has been lured out.

We approach the oligarchs of Torone directly, not as supplicants but as architects of their survival: we offer a binding treaty guaranteeing their property, reduced tribute, and continued internal autonomy under Athenian hegemony, on the single condition that they themselves eject the Spartan harmost and his garrison before our fleet arrives. This converts their fear of Brasidas into a fear of being caught between two vengeful powers, compelling a swift, self-interested betrayal. The bet is that the oligarchs, calculating that Brasidas cannot arrive before an internal coup is complete, will act preemptively to present Athens with a fait accompli, thereby securing our sea-lanes without a single marine landing and insulating the strategos from charges of reckless initiative.

The strategos declares a sacred armistice, summoning every Chalcidic polis—oligarchs and democrats—to an Amphictyonic congress at Apollo’s sanctuary at Poteidaia, casting Athens as founder of a new common peace. He dispatches heralds immediately, ensuring the summons reaches Brasidas within a day, and pledges his fleet to safeguard the delegates. The bet: Brasidas, bound by religious convention, must either honor the truce and halt his advance, ceding momentum, or breach it and alienate the Chalcidic cities, isolating himself without a battle.

judge.note: Strategy 4 lacks operational detail on herald timing, sanctuary logistics.

judge6.note: Strategy 3 (oligarch co-option) slightly baroque; Strategy 4 (amphictyonic congress) risks overestimating religious authority's binding force on Brasidas.

theme: spending the state's last RESERVES on one decisive campaign vs husbanding them for a long war of attrition
problem: You are the Athenian commander entrusted with the city’s last reserve of triremes and silver in 412 BC, after the Sicilian disaster. The Spartan fleet, backed by Persian gold, is gathering to sever Athens’ grain lifeline through the Hellespont. Should you commit these final resources to an immediate, decisive naval battle to destroy the Peloponnesian fleet before Persia fully arms them, or preserve them to defend the grain route and endure a long war of attrition, knowing that time strengthens the enemy’s alliance?
facets: The Peloponnesian fleet is inexperienced but growing; Persian treasure could turn it into an overwhelming force within months.
Allied states, shaken by the Sicilian loss, will interpret a defensive posture as weakness and may revolt, costing tribute and bases.
A decisive defeat at sea would leave Athens defenseless, while a slow attritional campaign risks gradual strangulation and political collapse.

angles.directive: Launch the entire fleet to surprise the Spartan naval base at Miletus, seeking to cripple their nascent fleet before Persian subsidies can finance a larger challenge.
Withdraw the triremes to the Hellespont, avoiding battle, and use the silver to fortify critical grain-route cities, forcing the enemy to fight far from their supply lines at a time of your choosing.

Send envoys with a portion of the silver to the Persian satrap Tissaphernes, attempting to outbid the Spartans and redirect Persian support toward Athens, neutralizing the naval buildup without a fight.

Use the last reserves to arm and transport a force of helot rebels and Athenian marines to establish a fortified base on the Spartan coast at Pylos, opening a second front that turns the war’s focus from sea lanes to the heart of Peloponnesian security.

threads: We will sortie every operational trireme under cover of darkness to fall on the Spartan squadron at Miletus while it is still half-crewed and its rowers untrained, ramming and boarding before they can form line. The Persian satrap Tissaphernes will interpret the sudden destruction of his nascent fleet not as a setback to be avenged but as proof that Athens still commands the sea, making further subsidies a risky gamble rather than a sure investment. That hesitation buys the tribute-paying allies the one thing that halts rebellion: the sight of Spartan hulls burning.

I will sail the reserve triremes directly to the Hellespont and use the silver to fortify Sestos and Byzantium, refusing all open-water engagements and anchoring our fleet behind these hardened chokepoints. The enemy is then forced into a dilemma: assault a dug-in fleet in narrow straits hundreds of miles from their own supply bases—a battle on our terms—or remain idle, burning Persian coin while their maritime coalition stagnates. That stagnation is the true weapon, as idle Spartan decks and unpaid allied crews will soon erode the credibility of their alliance and push wavering cities back toward Athens.

I will dispatch an envoy with a third of the silver directly to Tissaphernes, framing Athens not as a supplicant but as a more reliable and less geopolitically threatening client than a Spartan-led Peloponnese freed from Athenian naval pressure. This bribe is structured as an initial installment contingent on the satrap withholding his subsidies from the Spartan fleet for a single sailing season, buying us time to crush the nascent Peloponnesian squadrons in detail before they can concentrate. The bet is that Tissaphernes’s self-interest will recognize a balanced, prolonged war as more profitable for his satrapy than a swift Spartan victory that would inevitably turn its ambitions toward Persian holdings.

Commit the last reserve of triremes and silver to transport a picked force of Athenian marines and armed helot rebels to seize and fortify Pylos on the Spartan coast, establishing a permanent insurgent stronghold. This converts Sparta’s deepest structural vulnerability—its helot population—into an active second front, forcing an immediate diversion of Peloponnesian naval and land assets to contain a homeland insurrection. The bet is that this internal security panic unravels Spartan-Persian naval coordination before the enemy fleet can be fully financed and crewed, buying Athens the weeks needed to secure the Hellespont lifeline without hazarding a single decisive battle.

judge.note: Strategy 3 assumes Tissaphernes acts rationally; Strategy 4 helot revolt may be fantasy.

judge6.note: Strategy 3 envoy-bribe mechanism underspecified; Strategy 4 helot logistics vague.

theme: a CIVIL-WAR faction's temptation to invite a FOREIGN POWER inside the walls to crush its domestic rivals
problem: As leader of the oligarchic faction in Corcyra, you hold the acropolis but the democrats are massing for a final assault; envoys from your secret backer, Sparta, offer to sail into the harbor tonight with a small force, but if and only if you open the gate yourself—risking that their intervention will unite the democrats with neutral merchant clans, provoke Sparta's rival Athens to send a fleet, and turn your victory into a proxy war fought on your city's corpse.
facets: Corinth and Sparta have promised aid, but Athenian triremes are anchored just a day's sail away at Naupactus.
The neutral merchant clans, essential to future order, hold the grain stores and the lower city—they will await the battle's outcome before committing.
Your own oligarchs are split: some see foreign troops as salvation, others as an indelible stain that will trigger endless cycles of revenge from the democratic rank and file.

angles.directive: Accept the Spartan offer and launch a pre-dawn purge—strike the democratic leadership before they can rally, kill enough to cripple their faction permanently, and present the fait accompli as an internal Corcyraean resolution despite the foreign boots on the ground.

Immediately open negotiations with the neutral merchant clans, offering them a governing council of shared power in exchange for their active militia support—making the democrats' assault too costly and demonstrating that a stable, broad-based regime can exist without any external power's help or the vengeance spiral this will cause.

Evacuate the acropolis under cover of darkness, retreating to the countryside to wage a raiding campaign—let the democrats exhaust their strength holding an empty citadel, the neutrals implore you to return as guarantor of order, and the Spartan-Athenian dynamic play out at sea while you wait, mobile and with clean hands.

Send a secret messenger not to Sparta, but to the Athenian commander at Naupactus, revealing your faction's ability to block the harbor to Peloponnesian ships—bargain a swift Athenian-mediated truce that incorporates oligarchs into the constitution under Athenian guarantee, reframing the civil war as an Athenian strategic asset rather than a Spartan proxy victory.

threads: I will open the sea gate to the Spartan force at the second night watch and guide them to the democratic leaders’ compounds for execution before dawn, eliminating anyone who resists. At first light, I will proclaim the purge a strictly Corcyraean reckoning with traitors, daring the neutral merchant clans to refuse grain when the city is already securely in oligarchic hands and the democratic rank and file are headless. Athens will learn that the coup is complete and choose not to waste ships on a fait accompli that would only ignite the proxy war they fear.

I immediately offer the neutral merchant clans a council with veto over grain distribution and a third of assembly seats, in exchange for their militia securing the lower city. This makes the democratic assault unsustainable and signals Athens that no Spartan intervention is needed, removing the pretext for their fleet. The bet: the merchants, dreading Spartan dominance over their trade, will seize this share of power now, isolating the democrats and forcing a surrender before external powers turn Corcyra into a battleground.

I order a silent evacuation of the acropolis at night, slipping into the countryside to wage a raiding campaign that severs democrat supply lines. This forces the neutral grain-holding clans, who prioritize stability, to reject the starving democrats and entreat us to return as the unsullied guarantors of order. Spartan and Athenian triremes then clash at sea over interests, not over our city's corpse.

You secretly send an envoy to the Athenian commander at Naupactus, offering to block the harbor to Spartan ships in return for an immediate Athenian-mediated truce that embeds the oligarchs in a new constitution under Athenian guarantee. Athens will accept, reframing the conflict as an Athenian strategic asset that denies Sparta a base and secures Corcyra's fleet without a battle. The bet is that the neutral merchant clans, holding the grain stores, will then enforce the settlement under Athenian naval protection, cutting off the democrats' supplies and forcing them to negotiate rather than storm the acropolis.

judge.note: Strategy 4 assumes Athens accepts; 1 underestimates merchant backlash risk.

judge6.note: Strategy 3 (evacuation) and 4 (Athens pivot) strain credibility.

theme: recalling a brilliant but DANGEROUS COMMANDER for trial mid-campaign vs leaving him in the field where he is winning
problem: Athens has recalled the brilliant but impious Alcibiades from exile and he has just shattered the Spartan fleet at Cyzicus, yet the ruling oligarchs demand his arrest and trial for old sacrileges, fearing his popularity will restore the democracy. You must decide whether to recall him in chains while the fleet's morale is at its peak, or leave him in command to win more victories and risk him turning his victorious forces against the city.
facets: The navy's sailors and officers are personally loyal to Alcibiades; removing him could trigger mass desertion, mutiny, or defection to the Spartans.
The Persian satrap Pharnabazus is watching for signs of Athenian disunity and will switch his financial support decisively to Sparta if the city plunges into infighting.
The narrow oligarchic regime of the Four Hundred is deeply unpopular; a direct assault on a victorious general could ignite a democratic uprising in Athens itself.

angles.directive: Dispatch a marine detachment with sealed orders to arrest Alcibiades at Samos and transport him to Athens for immediate trial and execution, while naming a loyal oligarch as the new fleet commander to impose discipline.

Send a board of inquiry to the fleet to conduct lengthy, public hearings on the charges without issuing an arrest, stalling until the campaigning season ends and hoping that further victories silence his accusers or that the fleet’s patience with him fades.

Invite Alcibiades to return not as an accused man but as a hero, offering him a full partnership in the Council’s decisions and a shared command, binding his personal following to the regime and converting his ambition into a pillar of the oligarchy.

Grant Alcibiades a new grand mandate: seek Persian gold and lead a pan-Hellenic expedition to liberate and federate the Ionian Greek cities under Athenian protection, turning him into the architect of a new maritime league and redirecting his energies entirely outward.

threads: Dispatch the marine detachment under Thrasybulus with sealed orders to seize Alcibiades at Samos during the victory feast, when his guard is lowest, and transport him to Athens for a swift execution on charges of profaning the Mysteries. This move bets that the fleet’s officers, momentarily leaderless and stunned, will accept the new oligarchic commander rather than defect, because their material dependence on Athenian pay and supply outweighs personal loyalty to a single man.

The oligarchs dispatch a five-man board of inquiry to the fleet at Cyzicus, armed with subpoenas and a mandate for extended public hearings but explicitly denied arrest powers, deliberately stretching proceedings until the autumn storms halt campaigning. This stalls any immediate rupture while compelling Alcibiades to pursue further victories to sustain his heroic indispensability, betting that a decisive follow-up—such as seizing Byzantium—will render the old impiety charges politically unprosecutable. Pharnabazus, observing Athenian discord channeled into legal theater rather than open mutiny, withholds a decisive shift of Persian subsidies to Sparta, buying the regime a winter to consolidate.

The Four Hundred will pass an emergency decree recalling Alcibiades not for trial but as a hero, installing him immediately with a Council seat and full co-strategos authority over the fleet alongside their own appointee. This preemptively satisfies the navy’s loyalty by making mutiny pointless—his elevation is a gift of the regime, not a concession to rebellion. The critical bet is that Alcibiades, invested with legitimate power, will turn his ambition outward to destroy Sparta rather than gamble it on civil war, locking his sailors to the oligarchy and signaling to Pharnabazus that Athenian unity makes financial defection to Sparta a losing wager.

We issue a formal decree transforming Alcibiades from a recalled criminal into *strategos autokrator* with a specific mandate to extract Persian subsidies from Pharnabazus and forge an Ionian maritime federation under Athenian hegemony, which channels his ambition away from regime change by giving him a project larger than Athens itself. The bet is that Pharnabazus, seeing Alcibiades now operating as Athens’ diplomatic architect rather than its internal destroyer, withholds Spartan funding to preserve flexibility with a potential new eastern power bloc.

judge.note: Strategies 3-4 assume Alcibiades' ambition redirects too cleanly; Persian calculus underspecified.

judge6.note: Strategies 3-4 conflate co-optation with reframing; Persian subsidy logic underspecified.

theme: launching a distant, glittering FOREIGN EXPEDITION that overextends the state vs consolidating strength at home
problem: You are an Athenian strategos in 415 BC, debating whether to dispatch the full fleet to conquer Syracuse—a glittering prize that could encircle Sparta and make Athens master of the west—or to keep the armada in home waters to guard the empire against Spartan attack and allied revolt; the right choice depends on anticipating how Sparta, Syracuse, Persia, and your own subject-allies will react over the next several moves.
facets: The Segestans promise vast silver but their temple treasure display may be a staged fraud; their actual wealth is unverified and reliance on it could leave the expedition bankrupt.

The Peace of Nicias is fragile; Sparta’s war party is restrained only by fear of Athenian naval power, and a large fleet absence could trigger an immediate invasion of Attica or a relief force to Syracuse.
Your Aegean subjects watch intently—any perception of overreach or defeat could ignite a cascade of revolts from Chalcidice to Ionia, encouraged by Persian satraps like Tissaphernes and Pharnabazus.

angles.directive: Launch the full armada immediately, strike Syracuse before winter, overwhelm its fleet by sheer force, and return before Sparta can mobilise—bet everything on speed, shock, and a decisive knockout.

Send only a small demonstration squadron to Segesta to honour the alliance, while keeping the main fleet at Samos; defer the grand expedition until you see whether Segesta actually pays, whether Sparta stays quiet, and whether Syracusan internal divisions widen.

Dispatch an embassy to Rhegium, Leontini, Camarina, and even Carthage, offering alliance and autonomy under Athenian protection; forge a Sicilian anti-Syracusan coalition without committing the whole fleet, then join with a moderate force only to tip the balance at the critical moment.

Reframe the expedition as a pan-Hellenic rescue of Greek Sicily from the barbarian Carthaginian threat and the tyrant Hermocrates; invite Spartan observers and Corinthian ships to share the glory, thereby decoupling Sicily from the Peloponnesian conflict and keeping your home waters beyond reproach.

threads: Dispatch the full armada under Lamachus to strike Syracuse’s Great Harbour before winter, ramming their fleet in a single massed *diekplous* while Alcibiades simultaneously turns the city’s disaffected democratic faction. The wager is that Syracuse’s oligarchs, caught off-guard and bereft of naval cover, capitulate within weeks—not months—allowing the fleet to return north before Sparta’s war party can convert the shock into an actual invasion of Attica.

We dispatch Lamachus with a single fast trireme squadron to Segesta, not for conquest but to force the promised silver into physical audit, while the main battle fleet sits at Samos. This smaller force is sufficient to expose the Segestan fraud without triggering the strategic panic a full mobilization would cause in Sparta. The key bet is that Sparta’s war party, denied the casus belli of an empty Piraeus, remains paralyzed by internal division, letting us choke off the entire expedition’s logic at its financial root before a single Athenian hoplite is committed.

Dispatch a small, fast embassy to Rhegium, Leontini, and Camarina, offering full autonomy under Athenian protection and immediate naval support against Syracuse, while simultaneously sending a secret envoy to Carthage proposing a non-aggression pact that grants them western Sicily. This commits only twenty triremes as a demonstration force now, preserving the main fleet at Piraeus to deter Spartan invasion. The bet is that a visible anti-Syracusan coalition forming without our full commitment will compel Sparta’s war party to delay, calculating that Athens retains ample reserve power to crush any Attic incursion.

I propose we announce the armada as a Pan-Hellenic deliverance of Sicilian Greeks from Carthaginian invasion and Hermocrates’ tyranny. We will invite Sparta to send observers and Corinth to contribute a squadron under joint command, with a share of spoils. Once Corinthian hulls join our line, any Spartan move against Attica becomes a sacrilege against a sacred Pan-Hellenic undertaking, splitting the Peloponnesian League and keeping Persian gold from rebels who would defy a united Greece.

judge.note: Strategy 4 assumes Corinth joins; Strategy 2 assumes Sparta paralyzed by division—both optimistic.

judge6.note: Strategy 4 relies on fantasy coalition-building; Persian gold assumption ungrounded.

theme: a besieged city's choice to SURRENDER on terms, hold out for a rescue that may never come, or break out by force
problem: You command a coastal city allied with Athens, besieged by a Corinthian-led force. Your Athenian relief expedition is delayed by winter storms and a Spartan blockade; food will run out in three weeks, a faction inside conspires to open the gates, and the besiegers offer lenient surrender terms. The right choice depends on anticipating whether the Athenian fleet arrives, whether Sparta will accept a realignment, and how the internal conspiracy evolves over the next few moves.
facets: Athenian triremes are gathered but face adverse winds and a Spartan squadron; they might arrive in six weeks—or not at all.
Provisions will sustain the population for twenty-one days under strict rationing, but any delay beyond that means starvation.
A known oligarchic faction inside the walls is in contact with the besiegers, threatening to open a postern gate during the next dark night.

angles.directive: Hold out, impose severe rationing, and set counter-intelligence to root out the traitors. Bet everything on the hope that the Athenian fleet will break through in time, accepting the risk of collective starvation if it does not.

Mass the best hoplites and launch a breakout at night toward the coast. Fight through the besiegers’ lines now, aiming to reach the Athenian naval scouts and abandon the city if necessary, destroying the enemy’s ability to trap you.

Accept the Corinthian offer of surrender on terms—giving hostages and tribute, but preserving the city’s population and walls. Defer resistance to a future moment when Athenian power recovers and you can re-enter the war from a stronger position.

Send a covert envoy to the Spartan commander, bypassing Corinth, and offer to become a Spartan allied port paying tribute. Exploit Spartan-Corinthian rivalry to split the besieging coalition, integrating the city into the Peloponnesian alliance on favorable terms that neutralized the immediate threat.

threads: We impose rationing calibrated to twenty-one days and deploy trusted marines to seal the postern gate, arresting the oligarchic ringleaders mid-contact with the besiegers’ runners. This shock arrests the conspiracy’s momentum and buys us at least one more week of internal control, forcing Corinth to recalibrate its timetable. I am betting that extended defiance, combined with visible Athenian naval mobilization, compels Sparta to reassess the cost of a protracted siege and withdraw its blocking squadron rather than risk escalation.

Launch a nocturnal sally with the city’s best hoplites to break the circumvallation at its coastal hinge, then fight a covering action while non-combatants follow to rendezvous with Athenian naval scouts. This move bets that the Corinthian commander, surprised by the direction and speed, will rush his reserves toward the sound of fighting, fatally thinning the landward cordon and exposing the oligarchic conspirators’ postern contact to collapse before they can act.

We accept the Corinthian terms now—delivering hostages and a year’s tribute—while instructing our Athenian proxenos to bury naval funds at the temple of Poseidon for reactivation. This buys the fleet six weeks to fight through rather than rescue a corpse, and pre-empts the oligarchic faction by robbing their coup of purpose. We are betting that a lenient settlement makes Sparta unwilling to garrison a compliant city, leaving our residual network intact for an Athenian-backed reversal next spring.

Dispatch a trusted envoy under cover of darkness to the Spartan nauarch, bypassing Corinth entirely, with a sealed offer to transfer the city’s alliance to Sparta as a tribute-paying port, effective immediately. The proposal is calibrated to trigger Spartan greed for a strategically sited harbor without a costly assault, deliberately exploiting the well-documented Spartan suspicion of Corinthian ambition. The critical bet is that Sparta will publicly accept the submission and order Corinth to stand down, fracturing the besieging coalition before the internal conspiracy can act.

judge.note: Strategy 4 assumes Spartan greed overrides coalition loyalty; risky.

judge6.note: Strategy 4 envoy mechanics underspecified; Strategy 2 sally timing assumes unrealistic surprise.

theme: recalling a brilliant but DANGEROUS COMMANDER for trial mid-campaign vs leaving him in the field where he is winning
problem: As Nicias, co-commander of the Athenian expedition against Syracuse, you receive a sealed order from the Assembly to arrest Alcibiades—your brilliant, volatile colleague whose charisma is winning over Sicilian cities—and send him back to Athens to stand trial for profaning the Mysteries. If you obey, you risk fracturing the army, losing crucial momentum, and perhaps driving his supporters to the enemy; if you refuse, you defy the state and invite charges of conspiracy.
facets: Alcibiades' own response—arrest may provoke armed resistance in camp or flight to Sparta, where he could offer his strategic knowledge to the enemy; a warning might turn him into a fugitive who still commands loyalty within the ranks.

Army cohesion and morale—the soldiers trust Alcibiades; his sudden removal could shatter discipline, while leaving him in power might cause political enemies in Athens to cut off reinforcements and supplies, dooming the campaign from afar.

Syracusan and Peloponnesian reaction—any leadership crisis or delay gives Syracuse time to reinforce its walls and call in Spartan aid, turning a fluid siege into a protracted stalemate that Athens cannot sustain.

angles.directive: Execute the order at dawn: have Alcibiades seized by loyal marines and put on the next ship, then immediately purge his partisans from key commands. This eliminates the political threat decisively, but you must anticipate and crush mutiny in the hours after the arrest, while improvising a new command structure to keep the siege on track.

Stall for time: send a fast trireme to Athens with a plea to delay the recall on military grounds, citing an imminent defection by a Sicilian city that only Alcibiades can secure. You keep him in the field under a cloud, gambling that a quick victory will shift the Assembly's mood before they issue a second, harsher order.

Privately show Alcibiades the order and swear an oath before the army that you will stand together. Propose a joint dispatch that takes shared responsibility, allowing him to return voluntarily after the first major success. This binds his personal ambition to your survival, turning a potential defector into a partner who owes you his vindication.

Reframe the arrest as a matter for the gods: call a full assembly of the expedition and have priests read the charges alongside omens. Ask the host to decide collectively if Alcibiades should remain pending a purification rite. This transforms a political command crisis into a religious test, insulating you from direct blame and possibly unifying the army behind a sacred verdict.

threads: I will have Alcibiades arrested at dawn by a picked unit of marines loyal to me personally, then immediately detain his known partisans and reassign their commands to reliable officers. I anticipate that his most radical followers will attempt a mutiny within hours, so I will preposition a reserve force to isolate and disarm any unit that refuses orders. The bet is that swift decapitation and visible resolve will shock the army into compliance long enough for me to launch an immediate assault on the unfinished Syracusan counter-wall, turning the crisis into momentum before Sparta can intervene.

I dispatch the swiftest trireme to Athens with a sealed appeal to defer the arrest order, claiming that a key Sicilian city's imminent defection hinges on Alcibiades’ personal negotiation. The Assembly, fearing blame for wrecking a near-bloodless victory, will likely grant a temporary reprieve. I am betting that within that window, a rapid success will transform Alcibiades from a political poisoned chalice into the expedition's irreplaceable linchpin, rendering any renewed arrest order a direct assault on the city’s own prospects in Sicily.

I will pull Alcibiades aside tonight, show him the arrest order, and then we swear a public oath of mutual loyalty before the army while dispatching a joint letter suspending his recall until our first major victory. His hunger for a vindicating triumph will bind him to our success, turning a potential defector into a commander fighting for his own return. The real wager is that a swift, united assault on Syracuse’s still-unfinished defenses, powered by his undivided commitment, preempts Spartan intervention and forces Athens to receive a victorious general rather than hunt a fugitive.

I will summon the entire expeditionary force to assembly, where priests will recite the Athenian charges of profanation alongside fresh omens taken from our sacrificial victims, then call for a sacred vote: shall Alcibiades remain in command after performing a purification rite? This transforms a political arrest into a divine judgment, making the army itself the arbiter of his fate. The bet is that Alcibiades, faced with a collective pious verdict, cannot defy it without losing the soldiers’ loyalty, thus neutralizing the risk of armed resistance or flight while preserving our momentum against Syracuse.

judge.note: Strategies 1 & 4 assume unrealistic compliance; 2 & 3 underestimate Athens' resolve.

judge6.note: Strategies 1, 3, 4 risk mutiny/defection. Strategy 2 assumes Assembly compliance.

theme: whether to keep a fragile, mistrusted PEACE TREATY or resume the war while the advantage still holds
problem: Nicias, having brokered the Peace of Nicias, must decide whether to keep the fragile treaty and trust Sparta to bring its defiant allies to terms, or to resume war while Athens still holds the 120 Spartan prisoners from Sphacteria and commands the seas.
facets: Sparta's key allies (Corinth, Boeotia, Megara) reject the peace and are rearming; they may drag Athens into conflict even if Sparta complies.
The prisoners are Athens' sole coercive lever over Sparta—returning them removes leverage, but keeping them indefinitely is a breach of faith that will be seen as resumption of war.
Athens' naval superiority is transient: Sparta is seeking Persian funding to build a fleet, and delay could erode the strategic advantage that currently makes war attractive.

angles.directive: Strike at Corinth's navy and Boeotia's army immediately, dismantling the recalcitrant allies before Sparta can intervene, and then present a fait accompli to Sparta backed by the prisoners.

Keep the peace formally but refuse to return the prisoners until Sparta first forces its allies to ratify; stall negotiations and let the treaty languish in limbo while Athens holds hostages, avoiding the onus of aggression.

Offer Corinth a separate defensive alliance, promising Athenian protection against Spartan coercion and a share in trade, thereby splitting the Peloponnesian League and bringing a former enemy into Athens' orbit.

Transform the peace into a grand Hellenic alliance by proposing a joint Athenian-Spartan expedition against Persia, using the prisoners' return as a goodwill gesture to forge common cause, redirecting martial energy away from intra-Greek strife.

threads:
The treaty is already a fiction. Corinth and Boeotia are rearming openly, and every day spent pretending otherwise allows them to reconstitute the military capacity that Athens paid for in blood at Sphacteria. We possess a transient asset—naval supremacy—and a wasting one—the prisoners. The logical certainty is to expend the latter to secure the former while both still coexist. The prisoners are not merely bargaining chips; they are the functional equivalent of a defensive glacis. Sparta will not march to relieve Corinth or Boeotia if it knows that taking the field guarantees the execution of the 120. That paralysis opens a narrow operational window.

Strike now. Land a strong expeditionary force from the fleet directly into Boeotian territory, bypassing the border forts, forcing their hoplite army to fight on ground of our choosing, at a time when their harvest has not been gathered and their levies are scattered. Simultaneously, dispatch the full weight of the Athenian navy to the Gulf of Corinth to bottle up and burn the Corinthian fleet at its docks, eliminating the one force that could contest the seas with Persian silver. Neither operation requires a protracted campaign; both require speed and coordination. Once the allies’ military bones are broken, Sparta awakens to a new strategic map. The recalcitrant states that prevented Sparta from complying with the Peace, and that would have dragged it into war on unfavorable terms, no longer exist as coherent powers.

Then present the fait accompli. Return to Sparta not as supplicants but as agents of order: we have enforced the treaty upon your rebellious allies, we still hold your men, and we now offer a peace that Athens dictates—Pylos remains ours, the prisoners remain hostages for the duration, and Sparta withdraws all claims of suzerainty over the neutralised Boeotia and Corinth. Sparta will grumble because it must, but its strategic mind understands one brutal arithmetic: a land war against Athens is impossible without the Boeotian cavalry and the Corinthian fleet. Both have been neutralised in a fortnight. The prisoners, untouched, are the guarantee. Sparta will swallow the settlement because the alternative is to face Athens alone with 120 of its finest bound for the quarries, no navy, no allied front, and Persia suddenly less interested in funding a loser. This is not a betrayal of the Peace; it is its only viable execution.

We freeze the treaty in a state of suspended animation: Athens formally observes the peace but retains the 120 prisoners, conditioning their release on Sparta delivering its allies’ ratification—a condition we know Sparta cannot meet without a war it will not fight. This shifts the burden of paralysis onto Sparta, forcing it to either fracture its own alliance by coercing Corinth and Boeotia or to accept the diplomatic limbo and lose face. The bet is that Sparta’s allies, seeing Athens hold hostages without firing a shot, will read the delay as weakness and overextend—provoking a breach that Athens can then answer from naval dominance without having struck first.

Athens must immediately dispatch an embassy to Corinth offering a permanent defensive alliance: Athenian naval protection against any Spartan coercion, guaranteed access to Athenian-controlled trade routes, and a mutual non-separate peace pledge. This move bets that Corinth’s deep-seated resentment of Spartan hegemony will outweigh its hatred of Athens, fracturing the Peloponnesian League from within before Boeotia and Megara can coordinate rearmament. The critical downstream reaction is that Sparta, cornered by the defection of its most critical ally, will be forced to either attack Corinth—triggering Athenian intervention under the alliance and justifying war on defensive terms—or capitulate, rendering the prisoner leverage a secondary asset.

Nicias returns the 120 Spartan prisoners immediately as a deliberate goodwill sacrifice, then publicly proposes a joint Athenian-Spartan expedition to liberate the Ionian Greeks from Persia, with Athens commanding the naval theater and Sparta the land campaign. This reframes the peace not as a fragile truce but as the foundation of a bipolar Hellenic empire, making Sparta a co-investor in the treaty’s survival by giving it a tangible spoils-driven stake. The move bets that Corinth and Boeotia, lacking both a navy to participate and a patriotic pretext to oppose a pan-Hellenic campaign, are diplomatically paralyzed and can no longer drag Sparta into a renewed intra-Greek war.

judge.note: Strategy 4 (Transform) assumes Persia cooperation; unrealistic. Strategy 2 underspecifies Spartan breaking point.

judge6.note: Strategy 1 assumes Sparta paralysis; 4 assumes Persian campaign viability. Both overreach.

theme: an able general operating FAR FROM HOME, with discretion to liberate or coerce cities beyond his orders' intent
problem: An Athenian general, dispatched with a fleet to secure wavering Ionian cities, must decide how to handle Notion—a city whose oligarchs tilt toward Sparta—before a Spartan relief fleet arrives; his choice will either deter cascade defections or trigger a wider war.
facets: The speed and readiness of the approaching Spartan fleet under Lysander, currently at Ephesus.
The loyalty of the Notian demos versus the oligarchs, and whether a democratic faction can be trusted.
The reaction of other Ionian cities like Clazomenae and Erythrae, which watch for signs of Athenian weakness or brutality.

angles.directive: Storm Notion immediately, execute the oligarchic leaders, and install a democratic garrison to make a fearsome example before Lysander can intervene.
Sail past Notion to Chios, gather reinforcements and intelligence, and let the Spartan fleet commit elsewhere before returning to isolate the city with superior force.

Offer the oligarchs Athenian protection, honors, and a formal alliance, allowing them to keep power in exchange for tribute and basing rights—turning a potential enemy into a client without bloodshed.

Secretly arm the Notian demos and promise Athenian support for a democratic uprising, letting the city's internal revolution oust the oligarchs while your fleet blockades only enough to prevent Spartan interference.

threads: We storm Notion’s walls at night with the fleet’s marines, drag the oligarchs from their homes, execute them in the agora at dawn, and immediately arm the demos as a garrison. The brutality’s shockwave reaches Clazomenae and Erythrae before Lysander’s sails, freezing their pro-Spartan cabals with the vivid threat that any defection invites identical annihilation before reinforcements arrive. Those cowed cities will then refuse Lysander’s fleet the pilots and provisioning he needs, forcing him to fight a draining contest on our fortified terms rather than igniting a cascade.

I will sail the fleet past Notion directly to Chios, absorbing its democratic garrison and debriefing local agents on Lysander’s exact readiness at Ephesus. Once Lysander interprets our absence as hesitation and dispatches his fleet north to awe Clazomenae, I will double back with reinforced triremes and seal Notion’s harbor before the oligarchs can relight signal fires. The gamble is that Lysander overextends into Ionian waters on a show of force, leaving Notion momentarily naked for a swift, quiet reduction that avoids alarming Erythrae.

The general dispatches an envoy to Notion’s oligarchs with a sealed offer: Athenian protection, honors, and a treaty confirming their rule in exchange for tribute and basing rights. This preempts Lysander, who arrives to find a client city already bound, forcing him to either accept the neutralization or fight without a friendly beachhead. The wager is that Clazomenae and Erythrae, seeing the oligarchs’ power intact, will conclude that accommodating Athens is safer than defecting, halting the cascade.

I will dispatch covert agents to Notion with arms and gold for the democratic faction, promising naval support if they rise, while our fleet screens off Ephesus to intercept any Spartan sortie but refrains from assaulting the city. The demos overthrows the oligarchs in a swift coup, presenting Sparta with a fait accompli devoid of overt Athenian intervention. Lysander, bound to avoid provoking full-scale war without clear aggression, will hold, and a self-liberated Notion will stiffen democratic resistance in Clazomenae and Erythrae against pro-Spartan oligarchies.

judge.note: Strategy 4 underestimates Lysander's willingness to intervene covertly.

judge6.note: Strategy 2 timing/coordination mechanics underspecified; Strategy 4 agent infiltration vagueness.

theme: accepting a charismatic envoy's promise of ALLIANCE AND REINFORCEMENT that may be a bluff or a trap
problem: You command Athens’ advance guard at Segesta, where a charismatic envoy promises boundless silver and alliance to draw the full invasion armada—yet you sense a glittering bluff. If you accept, you may lead Athens into a ruinous trap; if you hesitate, Segesta could turn to Carthage or Syracuse, and your enemies in the Assembly will brand you a coward. Anticipate how rival cities and domestic politics will shift with your choice.
facets: The Envoy’s Performance: His gold might be borrowed, his loyalty a mask for Syracusan intrigue—discerning truth from theatre risks thousands of Athenian lives.
The Syracusan Shadow: Syracuse watches every hesitation; overcommitment could unite the whole island against you, yet weakness invites an immediate enemy thrust.
Athenian Politics at a Distance: The Assembly’s mood is a fickle wind—your choice becomes a weapon for demagogues at home, making you either empire’s sharpest blade or its perfect scapegoat.

angles.directive: Arrest the envoy on suspicion of fraud, seize Segesta’s treasury by force to expose the truth, and compel immediate compliance—daring Syracuse to react while signaling to Athens that you have preempted a trap.

Stall for time: insist the promised silver be delivered to your ships beforehand, send covert agents into the countryside to verify wealth, and prolong negotiations—delaying the armada until you are certain, all while keeping the envoy convinced of your trust.

Play the willing ally but bind Segesta tightly: demand their noblest youths as hostages aboard your triremes and require the silver be stored in a neutral temple under joint guard, making their bluff too costly to sustain and gradually drawing them into genuine dependence.

Reframe the entire mission on the spot: announce that Athens will not rely on uncertain local promises but will instead seize and fortify a smaller, vulnerable harbour as a self-funding base, turning a potential trap into an independent bridgehead for power in Sicily.

threads: I arrest the envoy mid-address, then force the treasury doors and publicly assay the silver with my own scales. The expected hollowness will be relayed to Athens as forensic evidence of a Syracusan plot, transforming the Assembly’s suspicion into a mandate for my preemptive strike. Syracuse, stripped of its deniable cat’s-paw and facing an entrenched Athenian garrison, will pause rather than escalate a war over a proven fraud.

I will demand the promised silver be physically transferred to my triremes before the armada sails, while covert agents fan out to verify Segesta’s mines and treasury, and I stretch negotiations with professions of unwavering trust. This stalls commitment without shattering the envoy’s facade, buying weeks for intelligence. The bet is that the envoy, cornered by a concrete delivery deadline, will either fabricate a default that unmasks him as a Syracusan puppet, or Syracuse will funnel real silver to maintain the trap—either outcome providing Athens with actionable proof and shifting the Assembly’s ire from my caution to the true enemy.

I will accept the alliance publicly but demand Segesta’s noblest youths as hostages aboard our triremes and require the promised silver be deposited under joint guard in the neutral temple at Eryx. This transforms their bluff into a cage: if the silver is borrowed, they cannot return it without exposure; if genuine, their elite become surety against betrayal. The move bets that Syracuse, seeing hostages already taken and silver secured, will read this as an irreversible Athenian entanglement and pause its aggressive designs, giving the Assembly time to authorize the full armada without panic.

I will reject Segesta’s gold, seize the vulnerable harbour of Hyccara, and fortify it as a self-funding base using slave labour and port taxes. This forces Syracuse—poised to crush an overland column—into a protracted siege against sea-supplied walls, splitting its coalition because neutral cities now see an Athenian counterweight to Syracusan tyranny rather than a foreign invader. Athens’ Assembly cannot brand a commander who delivers a fortified bridgehead a coward; the demagogues’ attacks shatter against strategic success.

judge.note: Strategies 1&3 assume envoy/Syracuse react predictably; 4 ignores Segesta's actual leverage.

judge6.note: Strategy 4 abandons the core problem; foresight assumes enemy passivity.

theme: a small NEUTRAL city pressured to SUBMIT to a stronger power or stake survival on justice and distant allies
problem: As an Athenian fleet sails to compel Hermione's submission, the city’s archon must choose now — fortify and trust Spartan relief, open the gates to Athens, turn Athens into a preferential ally, or hallow the land to Demeter. The right course turns on anticipating Spartan resolve, Athenian greed, and the weight of sacred oaths in a war of imperial ambitions.
facets: Sparta’s willingness to actually march forces into a coastal siege dominated by Athenian naval supremacy
Whether Athens would value a voluntary, equal ally offering a strategic harbor over a crushed tribute-paying subject
The credibility of a sacred pan-Hellenic truce given Athens’ history of treating piety instrumentally and the influence of the Delphic oracle

angles.directive: Fortify the walls and prepare for a long siege, counting on Sparta’s promised hoplite army to strike the Athenian besiegers from the rear before the city’s grain runs out; success depends on correctly judging whether Spartan honor plus geographic interest outweigh their fear of Athenian sea power.

Herald the gates open and accept Athenian tribute status now, sparing the population immediate slaughter; the gamble is that Athens will remain a predictable, lawful master rather than an ever-harsher imperial enforcer once resistance is absent.

Send envoys to the Athenian fleet offering to become a voluntary, allied naval station with a seat in the Delian League but no tribute — trading Hermione’s autonomy for a privileged partnership, wagering that Athens prizes strategic symbols of voluntary union over another broken subject.

Proclaim the whole territory a sacred temenos of Demeter and appeal to the Amphictyonic League for a pan-Hellenic truce, reframing any attack as sacrilege; success hinges on whether other poleis and the Delphic priesthood will enforce the taboo strongly enough to give Athens pause.

threads: We fortify the walls and dispatch a cutter to Gythium immediately, carrying not a plea but a hard strategic datum: Hermione’s harbor is the last deep-water anchorage on this coast not under Athenian oars, and losing it cedes the Argolic Gulf completely. The bet is that Sparta’s council will recognize this loss as a permanent encirclement worse than the risk of a naval engagement, and will therefore commit the hoplite column before our grain runs out, timing their march to catch the Athenian siege lines inverted against our walls.

The archon sends a herald to open the gates and accept immediate tribute-paying subject status, surrendering before the Athenian fleet even demands it, sparing the city siege and massacre. Athens, obtaining a valuable harbor without bloodshed, will likely administer Hermione with the routine, fixed tribute of the Delian League and minimal interference, because a model voluntary submission is propaganda more useful than a sack. The wager is that imperial greed will follow its own cold logic of rewarding easy conquests with a predictable, light hand rather than an escalating punitive spiral.

The archon sends envoys to the Athenian fleet with a precise offer: permanent allied naval station, Delian League seat, zero tribute, internal autonomy preserved in exchange for basing rights. Athens seizes the deal because a voluntary strategic harbor legitimizes its empire as a coalition of equals, sparing it a siege and the cost of garrisoning a hostile subject. The critical downstream bet is that this bloodless integration fractures Spartan cohesion—Peloponnesian neighbors, seeing Hermione’s walls standing and its honor intact, will calculate that defection is safer than resisting, leaving Sparta isolated.

We hallow Hermione’s entire chora to Demeter, invoke the Amphictyonic League, and demand Delphi issue a pan-Hellenic truce that brands any violation as sacrilege. The move forces Athens into a costly pause not because Pericles fears the gods, but because breaching a Delphic-sanctioned temenos would fracture his coalition—especially Corinthian and Plataean goodwill—and hand Sparta a propaganda weapon to rally neutral cities under the banner of defending sacred law.

judge.note: Strategy 4 relies on Delphi's political leverage; plausible but assumes oracle's willingness.

judge6.note: Strategy 4 (sacred reframing) strains credibility; others solid.

theme: spending the state's last RESERVES on one decisive campaign vs husbanding them for a long war of attrition
problem: You are the strategos advising the Athenian Assembly after the Sicilian disaster, with the city’s last reserve of 1,000 consecrated talents and a Spartan fleet blockading the Piraeus. Unleash the hoard on a single knockout campaign to crush the enemy and restore the empire, or preserve it to endure a protracted war of attrition, hoping that Persian gold, allied defections, and enemy exhaustion will eventually turn the tide. The right choice depends on anticipating how Sparta, Persia, and the wavering subject cities will move over the next year.
facets: The 1,000 talents are sacred emergency funds, never to be touched unless the enemy sails into the Piraeus; spending them now may break a religious taboo and demoralize the pious, but preserving them risks the city falling before they can be used.

The Persian satrap Tissaphernes is financing the Spartan fleet, but his subsidies are erratic and he may be playing both sides; his continued support could be short-circuited by a rival satrap or a change in the Great King’s policy.

The Athenian citizen body is fractious and exhausted: an oligarchic faction waits for a chance to seize power if the war drags, while a crushing defeat would shatter morale and trigger mass defections among the remaining allies.

angles.directive: Spend the reserve now to build a fleet and force a decisive naval battle off the Piraeus, aiming to annihilate the Spartan squadron, lift the blockade, and restore Athenian sea-power in one stroke, gambling that victory will cow the allies and stop Persian payments to a defeated Sparta.

Preserve the sacred hoard, withdraw the fleet into the fortified harbor, and rely on the Long Walls and grain imports to wait out the siege, deferring any major battle until the Peloponnesian allies quarrel over the cost of the war or Persian support runs dry, even if the city suffers famine and political unrest.

Use the thousand talents to bribe Tissaphernes and the leading men of Chios and Miletus, bringing the Persian paymaster and Sparta’s chief naval allies into the Athenian alliance; this would turn the enemy’s own financial and naval resources against them, isolating Sparta without a single decisive battle and converting the war into a contest of rival bids.

Invest the reserve not to defend Attica, but to dispatch a new expedition to the Hellespont to seize Byzantium and control the grain route, transforming the conflict from a defensive siege of Athens into a counter-siege that starves the Peloponnese, forcing the Spartans to abandon Decelea and fight on Athenian terms in a distant theater.

threads: The strategos immediately draws the full reserve to lay down and crew two hundred triremes within the month, then sorties to force a close-quarters battle off the Piraeus, using the narrows to negate Spartan maneuver. Annihilation of the blockading squadron lifts the siege and restores Athens’ sea-lanes, enabling grain imports and communication with remaining allies. Tissaphernes, seeing his naval proxy shattered and Athenian power resurgent, will freeze Persian subsidies to Sparta, while wavering subject cities interpret the victory as proof that Athens still punishes rebellion, halting defections for at least a season.

We withdraw the fleet behind the Piraeus booms and seal the Long Walls, converting the hoard into a strategic patience fund that purchases grain through neutral Megarian merchants while the Spartan blockade consumes itself on station. I am betting that Tissaphernes, seeing Athens neutralized but intact, will throttle his subsidies to deny Sparta a decisive victory and preserve the balance of power, triggering a funding crisis that fractures the Peloponnesian coalition before our bread runs out.

Dispatch emissaries directly to Tissaphernes with 600 talents to purchase his immediate defection, and allocate the remaining 400 to oligarchic factions in Chios and Miletus to deliver their fleets intact. The bet is that Tissaphernes, whose subsidies are already unreliable, will seize a single enormous payment over the protracted burden of financing Sparta, and that these Ionian elites value their own survival above the Peloponnesian alliance. Once Persian paymasters and the rebel fleet-commanders switch sides, Sparta’s blockade starves itself into collapse within the year.

We unleash the consecrated reserve to equip a fleet and send it under Thrasybulus to seize Byzantium, closing the Hellespont to Peloponnesian grain shipments. This instantly shifts the war’s center of gravity: instead of enduring a siege, we impose one, starving the enemy out of Decelea. The Spartans’ only rational response is to abandon the Piraeus blockade and race to the straits, where Athenian triremes, fighting in familiar narrows, can destroy their fleet and shatter their alliance.

judge.note: Strategy 3 assumes Tissaphernes defects; Strategy 4 assumes Sparta abandons blockade.

judge6.note: Strategy 3 (bribery) and 4 (Byzantium) rest on optimistic Persian/elite behavior.

theme: a CIVIL-WAR faction's temptation to invite a FOREIGN POWER inside the walls to crush its domestic rivals
problem: As the leader of the popular faction holding the eastern heights of a divided city, you watch Athenian triremes anchor offshore while your oligarch rivals have summoned Corinthian envoys; your next move must anticipate how Athenians, Corinthians, and your own wavering followers will react—do you invite the foreign power inside the walls or choose another path?
facets: The immediate survival of your faction versus the long-term loss of autonomy under Athenian hegemony.
The race between Athenian intervention and Corinthian reinforcement, which could transform the city into a proxy battlefield.
The fraying unity among your supporters—some would embrace Athens as savior, others would rather die than trade one master for another.

angles.directive: Launch a predawn assault on the oligarchic citadel, gambling that surprise and numbers will secure victory before Corinthian sails appear.
Withdraw your fighters and their families to fortified positions in the mountainous interior, ceding the city but preserving strength for a drawn-out insurgency.
Offer an immediate armistice, proposing a council evenly divided between factions and the exile of only the most extreme leaders to reunite the city against outside meddling.
Signal the Athenian squadron to send marines through the sally port at night, accepting their alliance and crushing the oligarchs in one stroke, whatever the future cost.

threads: I commit every armed supporter to a ladder assault on the oligarchic citadel two hours before dawn, massing at three breach points to overrun the garrison with shock and numbers before Corinthian sails are sighted. The gamble is that annihilating the rival leadership before sunrise presents the arriving Athenians with a consolidated democratic regime in control of the entire city, not a fractured client. Athens will then shield us from Corinthian retribution as a stable ally, not absorb us as a garrisoned dependency.

I order a full withdrawal tonight—every fighter, their families, and all portable supplies—to the pre-stocked redoubt at Koryphas, leaving the city gates open and the harbor unwatched. This move abandons the urban center to the arriving Athenians and Corinthians, who will now confront each other directly over an empty shell. I am betting that their mutual suspicion ignites a skirmish before either can secure the walls, turning the city into a contested no-man’s-land that bleeds both while my force survives intact to dictate terms later.

I offer the oligarchs an immediate armistice: an evenly divided council, exiling only the most extreme from both factions. The move fractures the oligarchic bloc by giving moderates a path to avoid Corinthian vassalage, while reuniting my wavering supporters under a patriotic refusal of foreign masters. It bets that without a clear invitation, Corinth will delay landing, forcing Athenian triremes to pause rather than escalate, and buying time to declare the city neutral.

We bring the Athenian marines through the sally port at midnight and guide them straight to the oligarchs’ assembly hall before Corinthian envoys can formalize their alliance, decapitating the rival faction in a single coordinated strike. The bet is that the Corinthians, seeing a *fait accompli* rather than a contested city, will stand down their reinforcement fleet to avoid a direct naval engagement with Athens for a prize already lost. This turns the race for external backing into an irreversible anchor that locks wavering supporters into compliance, since abandoning the victors now means facing Athenian retribution alone.

judge.note: Strategy 4 assumes Corinthian stand-down too readily; underweights naval escalation risk.

judge6.note: Strategy 4 requires trusting Athens utterly; Strategy 2 assumes predictable enemy collision.

theme: holding a conquered FREE REPUBLIC long accustomed to self-rule
problem: After seizing a free republic, a prince must act against exiled leaders raising an army and secret assemblies within—but any sudden strike risks uniting the opposition and drawing in foreign powers, while delay lets liberty rekindle.
facets: The depth of republican sentiment among the populace: some yearn for liberty, while others are weary of the old elite's mismanagement.
The speed and funding of the exiles' mercenary buildup: they may be months away from a credible threat, or weeks.
The ambitions of neighboring lords: whether they see the exiles as a useful tool to weaken the prince, or as a dangerous precedent for their own restive subjects.

angles.directive: Launch a night raid to scatter the exiles' camp and arrest the secret assembly's ringleaders, then hold a public trial to justify the purge as defense against foreign meddling. Anticipate that shock will buy a season of compliance, but watch for survivors to flee and rally a broader league against you.

Pull the garrison back to the citadel and the countryside, leaving the city to govern itself under a puppet mayor; let the exiles return, then crush them in a pitched battle when they are overconfident and the citizens' loyalties are tested. Anticipate that the republicans will overplay their hand, but beware that a successful initial uprising could attract the neighboring duchy's full army.

Summon the leading citizens and a delegation of the exiles to negotiate a new constitution that grants them seats in a princely council while keeping the prince as 'Protector.' Grant them honors and a stake in the tax farm. Anticipate that shared self-interest will cool republican fervor, but watch for the risk that they use their new platform to build a legal opposition and eventually demand real sovereignty.

Abolish the old assembly entirely and refound the city as a ducal seat under a new name, transferring the market and privileges to a loyal colony of your own veterans settled within. Recast the republic's memory as a period of corruption and faction, celebrating the prince's order-bringing conquest in civic rituals. Anticipate that erasing the symbols of liberty will make self-rule unimaginable in a generation, but beware that the displaced citizens may become a permanent diaspora with a grudge.

threads: The night raid on the exiles’ camp and simultaneous arrest of assembly ringleaders must be executed as a single shock operation—masked men seizing known agitators before dawn, while a fast column scatters the half-formed mercenary levy before it can concentrate. The public trial will frame the purge as foiling a foreign-funded conspiracy, not suppressing domestic liberty, thereby splitting the weary populace from the old elite by giving the former a patriotic justification for compliance. The critical bet is that the survivors who flee will overreach: their appeal to neighboring lords will trigger a defensive pact against republican contagion, hardening those lords into tacit allies who police the exiles for us rather than bankrolling a restoration.

I withdraw the garrison to the citadel and the ring of fortified villages, leaving a straw mayor to run the city under my seal. When the exiles sail in and proclaim the republic restored, I offer no resistance and let them hold assemblies that reveal which quarters and guilds arm for liberty. My wager is that a fortnight of uncontested rule will convince the neighboring duke the republic is viable enough to commit his full army—whereupon I envelop and annihilate that expeditionary force together with the exiles in a single pitched battle outside the gates.

I will convene a constitutional congress of leading citizens and exile delegates, granting Council seats and tax-farm shares under my Protectorship. The bet is that this fractures the opposition: office-hungry moderates will break from irreconcilable radicals, whom I can then crush as marginal extremists. Neighboring lords, seeing the exiles split and negotiating, lose the pretext of restoring a legitimate republic and hesitate to intervene.

The prince dissolves the old assembly, renames the city as a ducal seat, transfers its market and privileges to a veteran colony settled within, and institutes annual triumphal rites casting the republic as an era of factional corruption. The bet is that neighboring lords, seeing the republic’s very name erased and its institutions replaced by a loyal, order-bringing fief, will refuse to fund the exiles’ mercenary buildup for fear of legitimizing a restored-republic precedent that could ignite their own restive subjects. The displaced citizens become a permanent, grudge-bearing diaspora, but without a territorial base or foreign patronage their cause atrophies.

judge.note: Strategy 2's pitched-battle gambit assumes duke commits fully; risky assumption.

judge6.note: Strategy 2 (pitched battle gambit) relies on implausibly precise enemy coordination.

theme: a CIVIL principality: a prince elevated by fellow citizens, balancing the nobles against the people
problem: Elevated by popular acclaim after the old duke's fall, you must decide how to redirect the city's long-building fury without alienating the wary lesser nobles whose silent cooperation you still need—before the neighboring condottiero returns from campaign.
facets: The people demand swift, visible punishment of the old regime's enablers, testing whether you are truly their champion.
The lesser nobles offer essential administrative continuity but fear mob justice will eventually consume them too.
The exiled condottiero retains a hardened mercenary company and a plausible claim to restore order through force.

angles.directive: Launch a spectacular public trial and execution of the old duke's three most hated ministers immediately, daring the nobles to object and forcing the condottiero to react before his forces are fully resupplied.

Appoint the most competent reformist noble as your chancellor and charge him with designing a tribunal that blends popular jurors with patrician judges, binding his faction's fate to the process's legitimacy.

Announce that justice will follow only after a city-wide census and written grievance registry is complete, buying months of bureaucratic delay to see whether the condottiero's foreign patrons renew his contract.

Reframe the crisis from 'punishing past villains' to 'securing future liberty' by proposing a citizen militia ordinance, making the political question about who will physically defend the city when the condottiero returns.

threads: I will convene a People’s Tribunal in the Ducal Square tomorrow, presenting irrefutable evidence of the three ministers’ crimes before a crowd of thousands, then execute them at sunset. This public sacrament of retribution compels the lesser nobles to visibly endorse the verdict or expose themselves as the mob’s next target, buying their terrified compliance. I am betting that the condottiero, seeing the city united in bloody spectacle, will accelerate his return and attack before securing supply lines, letting my prepared militia ambush his exhausted column at the river ford.

Appoint Lord Adriano, the most capable reformist noble, as Chancellor and task him with forming a tribunal where popular jurors sit alongside patrician judges, making his faction co-owners of the verdicts. This forces the lesser nobles to accept the process as a shield against mob excess, since their peer now controls its design. The bet is that the condottiero’s “restore order” claim collapses when the city demonstrates it can deliver calculated justice without him.

I will announce a city-wide census and formal grievance registry as the non-negotiable prerequisite to any tribunal, appointing the lesser nobles themselves to administer its collection, thereby transforming popular fury into a protracted paper siege that buys months of delay. This bureaucratic theater preserves administrative continuity by making the nervous nobles indispensable gatekeepers of the very process they dread, while the real clock runs on the condottiero’s foreign contract. The bet is that his distant patrons, observing a stable city absorbed in legal procedure rather than mob chaos, decline to renew his commission, stripping him of the paid company he needs to force a restoration.

I will immediately propose a Citizen Defense Ordinance that raises a city militia from the popular ranks but grants officer commissions exclusively to the lesser nobles, tying their status directly to the new order's military readiness. This reframes all political energy around the concrete question of who holds the walls when the condottiero returns, converting the demand for vengeance into a disciplined armament drive. The bet is that nobles, seeing command over an armed citizenry as a surer shield against both exile retribution and mob lawlessness than passive neutrality, will compete for these posts—inadvertently legitimizing my rule and confronting the condottiero with a fortified, united city rather than a fractured one ripe for "restoration."

judge.note: Strategy 1's river-ford ambush assumes condottiero predictability; Strategy 3's patron-abandonment assumes distant political calculation.

judge6.note: Strategy 1 relies on mob coercion of nobles; Strategy 4 assumes nobles compete for militia posts.

theme: rewarding the OVER-MIGHTY NOBLE whose aid won the throne but who now rivals the prince
problem: Duke Orsini’s private army placed Prince Lorenzo on the throne, but Orsini now demands hereditary rule over half the realm’s cities. If Lorenzo refuses, Orsini may march on the capital; if he grants the demand, he creates a rival state within the state. Meanwhile, the Republic of Siena probes the borders, and his own mercenary captains will judge his next move as a signal of strength or weakness.
facets: The duke’s network of client cities and mercenary captains is personally loyal to him, not to the prince’s state.
The Republic of Siena is actively seeking a pretext to intervene and would back any pretender who emerges from a civil war.
The treasury is depleted from the war of conquest, so the prince cannot simply buy the duke’s loyalty or quickly raise new forces.

angles.directive: Strike first: fabricate evidence of sedition, execute the duke publicly, and purge his captains before they can coordinate. Time the blow to coincide with a false rumor of Sienese aggression, framing the duke as a foreign agent caught in treachery.

Send the duke on a prestigious embassy to the Emperor to negotiate a mutual defense pact, keeping him far from his power base for months. Meanwhile, quietly garrison his key cities with soldiers loyal to the prince, using the duke’s absence to dilute his influence.

Publicly adopt the duke as your heir and co-ruler, granting him a crown and integrating his household into the royal court. Any move against you would then become a move against his own future throne, aligning his ambition with the state’s survival.

Appoint the duke as governor of the contested border march with Siena, with the authority to raise his own troops and keep any conquests. Let his ambition feed on the republic’s lands, forcing him to exhaust his private army against an external enemy while you consolidate the heartland.

threads: Prince Lorenzo will summon Duke Orsini to a privy council, produce a forged letter detailing his “negotiations” with Siena, and execute him on the spot while his captains are seized in simultaneous raids. The strike is timed with a fabricated border skirmish report, framing the duke as a foreign agent caught in mid-treason. The bet is that the duke’s client cities, suddenly decapitated and confronted with damning evidence of his betrayal, will fracture into paralysed, self-interested fragments, allowing Lorenzo to install loyalist governors before Siena can convert the chaos into a formal incursion.

Prince Lorenzo names Duke Orsini ambassador plenipotentiary to the Imperial court, charging him with negotiating a mutual defense pact—an honor he cannot refuse without spurning the Emperor. During his months-long absence, loyalist troops quietly relieve Orsini’s city garrisons, citing the need to counter Sienese border probes, and replace key officers. The strategy banks on Orsini’s dispersed condottieri hesitating to act without their patron’s direct command, allowing the new garrisons to consolidate control and sever the duke’s personal grip before he can return to coordinate a rebellion.

Lorenzo adopts Orsini as co-ruler and heir, formally investing his client cities as crown fiefs managed by Orsini’s retinue within an integrated court. The key bet: Orsini’s private army will pivot to repel Siena’s probes, because attacking Lorenzo would now destroy the succession that guarantees his own house’s royal future. This display of unified strength convinces the mercenary captains of resolve, deprives Siena of a pretender, and merges loyalist resources to offset the empty treasury.

Name Orsini Lord Warden of the Sienese Marches with letters patent to raise his own troops and keep every fortress he takes from the Republic. His mercenary captains, scenting plunder beyond the border, will follow him into a grinding war that Siena must escalate to defend its territory, locking his personal army into an attritional stalemate.
