While he exhausts his client cities’ manpower and treasure against an external foe, I rebuild the royal fisc and quietly replace his garrison commanders in the heartland with officers loyal to the throne.

judge.note: Strategy 1 assumes forged letter credibility; Strategy 3 underestimates Orsini's ambition.

judge6.note: Strategies 1&2 assume perfect coordination; 3&4 underestimate Orsini's agency.

theme: whether to be FEARED or LOVED by newly acquired subjects
problem: You have just annexed a wealthy city-state whose citizens despise your rule but whose merchant guilds control trade routes your rivals covet; a public display of mercy now could invite a coup by exiled nobles, yet exemplary cruelty could drive the guilds to fund a rival's invasion next season.
facets: The exiled nobles are already in talks with a stronger neighboring prince, but he will only back them if the city's internal unrest makes occupation costly.
The merchant guilds can shift their financial loyalty within weeks, and whichever prince they fund will gain a decisive advantage in the coming campaign.
The common citizens are watching your first public judgment of a captured conspirator—a beloved local figure—which will set the narrative about your reign for the next year.

angles.directive: Appoint the merchant guild leaders to your privy council with genuine tax authority, binding their wealth to your regime's survival while publicly pardoning the conspirator in their name—so mercy appears as their gift, not your weakness.

Delay any public judgment indefinitely by announcing a grand tribunal to 'thoroughly investigate' the conspiracy, keeping the conspirator imprisoned but alive, which buys months to first negotiate a trade treaty with the rival prince that makes his invasion economically ruinous.

Stage the conspirator's confession as a conversion to your cause, then send him as your personal envoy to negotiate terms with the rival prince—reframing him from martyr to collaborator and forcing the rival to either reject diplomacy or accept a peace that undercuts the exiles.

Execute the conspirator immediately and publicly, then seize the moment of shock to declare the merchant guilds' assets forfeit unless each guild master personally swears fealty within three days—using their fear to break their collective bargaining power before they can coordinate with the rival prince.

threads: I appoint the three senior merchant guildmasters to my privy council, granting them genuine authority over customs duties—their wealth now depends on my regime's survival. I will publicly pardon the conspirator tomorrow explicitly as a mercy granted at the guilds’ petition, transferring the credit to them. The wager is that the guilds, financially bound and publicly credited, will immediately deny the exiled nobles the funds needed to make the city appear ungovernable, collapsing the neighboring prince’s intervention rationale before he commits.

I declare a grand tribunal to “thoroughly investigate” the conspiracy, holding the beloved conspirator alive and delaying any judgment indefinitely. Simultaneously, I dispatch envoys to the rival prince with a proposed trade treaty that grants his merchants privileged, exclusive access to the guilds’ routes, contingent on a neutrality pledge—any invasion annuls the pact and triggers ruinous trade sanctions. The bet is that the prince, facing a binary choice between a guaranteed economic windfall and a costly, sanction-hit occupation, will defect from the exiled nobles, leaving them unfunded and the guilds locked into my orbit.

I publicly stage the conspirator's confession as a genuine conversion, then immediately send him as my peace envoy to the rival prince, carrying trade concessions that signal guild-friendly intent. This transforms the city's potential martyr into a visible collaborator, evaporating the exiles' narrative before the commoners and making guild defection less patriotic. The rival must either reject the gesture—branding himself a warmonger and unifying the city against him—or accept negotiations, which forces him to sideline the exiled nobles and cede the trade routes to my de facto control.

I will publicly behead the conspirator at dawn, then declare all guild assets forfeit unless each master kneels and swears personal fealty within three days. The shock fractures their collective will, forcing individual masters to salvage their fortunes rather than coordinate with the rival prince. The rival prince, seeing the guilds neutralized, will withhold support from the exiled nobles, buying me a season to consolidate.

judge.note: Strategies 2-4 assume rival prince behaves rationally; ignore domestic guild fracture risks.

judge6.note: Strategy 4 relies on coercion collapse; strategies 2-3 assume rival prince's rational defection.

theme: a CIVIL principality: a prince elevated by fellow citizens, balancing the nobles against the people
problem: A prince raised by the people to curb noble abuses discovers that several leading nobles are conspiring with a neighboring duke to overthrow him. The nobles control vital military commands and tax collection; crushing them openly could trigger civil war and invite invasion, yet tolerating the plot risks a coordinated coup. He must choose how to act now to shape the next sequence of reactions from the nobles, the foreign duke, and the common people.
facets: The military and administrative dependence on the conspiring nobles, making a rapid purge risky without a credible replacement structure.
The deep popular support that can be mobilized as a counterweight, but only if the prince can channel it without provoking a preemptive foreign intervention.
The rival duke's calculus of opportunity: he will invade if he sees a fractured state, but will abandon the conspirators if they become liabilities.

angles.directive: Preemptively arrest all implicated nobles before they can coordinate, execute them publicly as traitors to the people, and immediately fill their commands with loyal commoners and minor gentry—betting that swift terror will deter the rest and that the foreign duke will not attack a state already on a war footing.

Summon the conspirators individually under the guise of favor, offer them elevated titles, prestigious marriages into your own house, and a share in planning a new external campaign—channeling their ambition into a joint enterprise that makes them hostages to your shared success.

Secretly leak forged evidence to the rival duke that the nobles intend to betray him once inside the city, while simultaneously warning the nobles that the duke plans to depose them after using their revolt. Let mutual suspicion destroy the conspiracy without your direct hand, then stand ready to reconcile with whichever side survives.

Proclaim a new civic order: arm every able commoner in a citizen militia, paid for by a special levy on noble lands, and declare that any noble who opposes this is an enemy of the people's liberty. Shift the very foundation of power so that noble military relevance dissolves, making their conspiracy irrelevant regardless of the foreign duke's intentions.

threads: He arrests every implicated noble in a single night using loyalist militias drawn from the urban quarters, tries them before a people’s tribunal the next morning, and executes them by dusk, then commissions literate commoners and reformist lower gentry into the vacated commands under a provisional wartime levy. The bet is that the foreign duke, seeing the prince’s regime instantly consolidate rather than fracture, will recalculate the conspirators as a burned asset and stand down rather than strike a mobilized state.

Summon the three most militarily powerful conspirators separately and announce a new “Eastern March” campaign against contested borderlands, granting each a hereditary ducal title over territories they must jointly conquer, while betrothing their heirs to your own cousins to bind their bloodlines to regime survival. This converts their coup incentive into a shared expansionist enterprise where defection to the foreign duke would forfeit their future fiefs and hostages. It bets that the rival duke will then abandon the conspiracy, calculating that invading a united realm led by his former co-conspirators—now your in-laws and military partners—is a losing proposition.

He leaks forged correspondence to the rival duke that frames the conspiring nobles as planning to seal the city gates and slaughter his retinue once he commits troops inside, while simultaneously routing doctored letters to the nobles exposing the duke’s secret intent to execute them and install his own governors. This twin deception collapses the conspiracy by redirecting their operational trust into paralyzing mutual fear. The bet is that the duke, seeing the nobles as a trap rather than an asset, withdraws his backing first, leaving the isolated nobles scrambling for a pardon that only the prince can grant.

The prince proclaims a citizen militia paid for by a levy on noble lands, branding any noble opponent an enemy of the people. This forces the conspirators to either submit—losing their armed retinues and tax-collection leverage—or openly defy the edict, instantly turning their local influence into a liability as armed commoners shift loyalty to the crown. The foreign duke, calculating that a unified popular army makes invasion costly and the conspirators are now useless as proxies, will abandon them to preserve his own borders.

judge.note: Strategy 2 assumes bloodline ties reliably override material incentives; Strategy 3 assumes deception holds under scrutiny.

judge6.note: Strategy 3 (forged letters) relies on deception too fragile; Strategy 1 risks backlash.

theme: a prince raised by his own CRIME or betrayal, now consolidating an illegitimate seizure
problem: A usurper who poisoned his master, the old duke, now rules in fear as the duke’s daughter rallies a rival king’s army. The nobles who witnessed the murder wait to see which side will triumph, and any misstep will tip them—and his own complicit inner circle—against him.
facets: The rival king will only commit his full army if the daughter can demonstrate substantial noble support, creating a narrow window to erode her credibility before the season for campaigning opens.
The nobles care above all for stability and their estates; they will throw their weight behind whichever claimant seems most likely to win and to protect their privileges.
The usurper’s own captains were accomplices in the poisoning—they are bound to him only by shared guilt and fear, and will defect the moment his fortunes appear to wane.

angles.directive: Send assassins to kill the daughter before she reaches the foreign court, eliminating the figurehead and demoralizing her backers, even though a failed attempt would accelerate the invasion.
Propose a marriage alliance that would merge her claim with his de facto power, offering to share the duchy if she renounces foreign aid, thereby absorbing her legitimacy and dividing her cause.

Fabricate evidence that the daughter conspired with the old duke’s enemies to sell the realm to foreigners, then hold a public trial (in absentia) to condemn her as a traitor, turning her from wronged heir to national threat.

Abandon the capital and withdraw to a defensible stronghold, letting the daughter and her foreign army occupy the city. Over time, the burdens of occupation and the nobility’s impatience will fray their alliance while you wage a war of attrition.

threads: I will dispatch our household poisoner, disguised as a wine merchant, to dose the daughter’s cup with nightshade at the final coaching inn before she crosses the border; her party will find her dead by morning. The rival king, stripped of a living claimant to legitimize his invasion, will withhold his army rather than shoulder the cost of a war with no figurehead. The nobles, reading her sudden death as fortune’s judgment, will immediately swear fealty to the surviving duke, freezing any defection calculus among our complicit captains.

I will send an emissary to offer her a marriage compact: co-ducal rule, half the council, and full estate restoration in exchange for publicly abjuring the rival king’s army. Accepting absorbs her legitimacy into my regime while I place loyalists on the council; rejection, which I will ensure is public, frames her as a foreign cat’s-paw prioritizing war over peace to every noble assessing the odds. The bet is that nobles will desert her en masse to avoid a campaign, and with no domestic standard raised, the rival king will withhold his main force.

We fabricate letters, seal them with a forgery of her personal cipher, and plant them with a dead foreign courier, then stage a public inquisition where bribed servants “discover” the correspondence proving she promised border provinces to the rival king in exchange for his army. The trial shifts the nobles’ perception of her from avenging daughter to reckless traitor willing to dismember the realm. This bets that the rival king, seeing her credibility collapse, withholds his full commitment rather than risk his army for a disgraced claimant whom the nobles now shun.

I will strip the treasury and my household guard from the capital, march them to the fortress at Ironholt, and cede the city without a skirmish, then refuse every pitched engagement her foreign army offers. The rival king’s soldiers, billeted on noble estates to supply themselves, will inevitably requisition grain and insult local privileges, transforming the daughter’s liberation into an occupation in the aristocracy’s eyes. That creeping resentment is the bet: it will freeze the nobles’ declarations, denying her the demonstrated support she needs to unlock the king’s full army, and eventually pressure her into a negotiated settlement that leaves my core power intact.

judge.note: Strategy 4 assumes noble resentment will crystallize; timing uncertain.

judge6.note: Strategies 1-3 are assassination/manipulation (illegal). Strategy 4 alone is realistic.

theme: the TIMING AND DOSAGE OF CRUELTY — all at once vs spread out — to pacify a disorderly new state
problem: A prince has just seized a fractious city-state boiling with noble conspiracies, armed guilds, and a populace still loyal to the exiled duke. To secure his new dominion before the neighboring kingdom can intervene, he must choose between a single overwhelming wave of terror that annihilates all likely enemies at once, or a gradual trickle of punishments that keeps the threat alive but avoids a spectacular shock—each path shaping the city’s stability, the loyalty of survivors, and the calculations of his foreign rival over the coming months.
facets: The neighboring kingdom’s intervention calculus: a swift mass purge could be seen as a sign of strength that deters action, or as an atrocity that provides a casus belli and a rallying cry.

The local elite networks: decapitating all potential leaders in one stroke may leave a headless but simmering resentment; piecemeal cruelty can keep factions divided yet constantly afraid, never knowing who is next.

Popular sentiment: a single, spectacular cruelty followed by immediate clemency and benefits can transmute fear into awe and gratitude, whereas drawn-out punishments risk embedding a lasting narrative of tyranny and hatred.

angles.directive: Launch a single, meticulously planned night of terror—arrest and execute every known conspirator, pretender, and their key relatives, then immediately declare a general amnesty, lower taxes, and bestow honors on the rest, betting that the shock will paralyze remaining resistance, the abrupt cessation of violence will restore calm, and your demonstrated ruthlessness will deter external foes before they can marshal a response.

Hold off on mass bloodshed; maintain an uneasy peace with minimal visible force while infiltrating the city with spies and informants. Let conspiracies mature and expose themselves over time, striking only when a genuine coup attempt materializes, hoping that delaying will allow your own military to consolidate, your rival next door to lose interest, and the original fervor of resistance to cool into fatalistic complacency.

Transform the fractious nobility into stakeholders by offering them titles, marriages into your own house, and lucrative offices in the new regime. Only those who obstinately refuse are quietly exiled or killed in isolation, framed as ungrateful outliers, so that cruelty appears as a reluctant, surgical necessity rather than a reign of terror, co-opting the elite while preserving a merciful public image.

Appoint a notoriously harsh viceroy with full authority to pacify the city by any means necessary, keeping your own hands formally clean. Once order is ruthlessly restored and the population’s hatred concentrates on the deputy, stage his spectacular trial and execution or banishment for ‘excesses,’ thereby channeling popular fury onto the scapegoat and presenting yourself as the magnanimous restorer of justice who has purged both the old disorder and its cruel remedy.

threads: The prince dispatches royal guard units in a single night to arrest and behead every documented conspirator, guild enforcer, and their immediate heirs—roughly two hundred names—then broadcasts a general amnesty, a ten-percent tax reduction, and distributes vacated estates to loyalist merchants. The shock decapitates all organized resistance at once, leaving no patron for the exiled duke’s return or for foreign intrigue. The neighboring kingdom, observing a city that is silent and orderly rather than bleeding, reads the purge as ruthless competence and refrains from intervention, calculating that there is no internal proxy left to support.

We seed the city with informants across guilds and noble houses, forgoing all public arrests so that discontent crystallizes into a single provable coup attempt before we strike. This deliberate restraint denies the neighboring kingdom the spectacular atrocity required to unify its court for intervention, while the unmolested factions, receiving no clear enemy, turn suspicion inward. The bet is that their coordination dissolves into paranoid feuding over who is already compromised, buying the months needed for our forces to consolidate and for external interest to wane.

I will offer every noble house a title, marriage alliance, and lucrative office in exchange for fealty, branding refusers as outlaws. Those who refuse are quietly executed, framed as isolated traitors who spurned reconciliation, their lands granted to loyalists. This wagers that the neighboring kingdom, lacking a massacre to condemn, will hesitate to intervene, while the ennobled collaborators, now invested in the regime, will suppress guild unrest and popular nostalgia for the exiled duke as a condition of their new status.

I secretly appoint General Vorenus as viceroy with a free hand for mass proscriptions and public terror, disclaiming responsibility. Once the city is pacified and its wrath fixed upon him, I will have him seized, publicly condemned for his ‘excesses,’ and executed alongside a festival of amnesty and bread. The calculation: the population’s hatred dies with his body, transferring their loyalty to me as the just cleanser, while the foreign rival, watching me disown the terror, loses the moral pretext for intervention precisely when the city is united behind my rule.

judge.note: Strategy 4 relies on untested assumption that scapegoating Vorenus cleanly transfers loyalty.

judge6.note: Strategy 4 (scapegoat execution) strains credibility; foreign powers recognize theater.

theme: rewarding the OVER-MIGHTY NOBLE whose aid won the throne but who now rivals the prince
problem: A newly crowned prince owes his throne to Duke Valerio, whose military strength and popularity now rival his own. If Valerio is left unchecked, he may usurp the crown; if moved against prematurely, his loyalists could ignite civil war while a predatory neighbor kingdom threatens the border. The prince must choose a course whose wisdom depends on whether Valerio's ambition festers, the neighbor attacks, or the people rally to a display of strength—or mercy.
facets: Valerio's personal temperament: Is he content with influence or coveting the throne?
The opportunistic neighboring kingdom that will seize on any sign of weakness or civil strife.
The silent majority of lesser nobles and the common populace, who weigh gratitude to the kingmaker against fear of a ruthless or weak ruler.

angles.directive: Accuse Valerio of plotting treason and strike decisively—arrest and execute him before he can rally his forces, counting on shock and speed to deter any uprising and present the neighbor with a united front.

Dispatch Valerio as a special envoy to the rival kingdom to negotiate an alliance or tribute, a mission so perilous and prolonged that his absence drains his domestic influence, while the prince fortifies the capital and wins loyalties.

Bind Valerio to the dynasty by marrying him to the prince's sister and installing him as chief minister, intertwining his fate with the regime's survival and turning his ambition into a stake in shared power.

Elevate Valerio to Viceroy of a wealthy but restive frontier province, framing it as a reward that matches his 'indispensable' talents, thereby isolating him from his core supporters and forcing him to exhaust his resources quelling distant unrest.

threads: The prince will summon Duke Valerio to a private royal audience, where a concealed detachment of the palace guard arrests him on a pre-drafted charge of high treason. He is executed within the hour, his body displayed to signal irreversibility. The move bets that leaderless hesitation among his loyalists will fragment their response, buying days to install loyal officers

The prince dispatches Valerio as special envoy to negotiate a critical alliance with the rival kingdom, stripping him from his power base under the guise of supreme trust. His prolonged absence allows the prince to garrison the capital with loyal troops, buy off lesser nobles, and frame every royal decree as urgent border defense. The entire maneuver bets that Valerio either refuses—branding him a traitor to the threatened realm—or accepts and watches his influence evaporate, leaving his followers leaderless and easily absorbed before he can return.

The prince will bind Valerio by marriage to his sister and appoint him chief minister, with command of the border forces. This chains Valerio’s honor to the dynasty: any move to usurp appears as kin-treason to the silent nobility, inviting the neighbor’s attack and his own ruin. His ambition then has no safe course but a victory over the neighbor—a success that cements him as the regime’s shield, rendering usurpation both unnecessary and self-destructive.

The prince will issue a public decree appointing Valerio as Viceroy of the turbulent, silver-rich Marches, framing the promotion as the only honor worthy of his indispensable military genius in pacifying chronic border raids. Stripped of his courtly power base and transplanted into a province where every local chieftain will test his authority, Valerio must bleed his personal treasury and loyal retainers in a grinding counterinsurgency that consumes his ambition. The neighboring kingdom, watching the frontier stiffen under a warlord now fighting for his own survival, will delay adventurism, while the silent nobles and populace, seeing a decisive prince who rewards service without fear, rally to the crown’s authority.

judge.note: Strategy 3 assumes honor-binding works; Strategy 4 assumes grinding exhaustion. Both optimistic.

judge6.note: Strategy 1 (execution) risks immediate civil war despite claims. Strategy 3 (marriage) assumes honor-binding works across ambition.

theme: securing HONEST COUNSEL while surrounded by flatterers, without eroding authority
problem: A newly installed prince must determine how to test the loyalty of his inherited council, knowing that purging too aggressively will isolate him from the realm's knowledge, while retaining flatterers will steer him into ruinous decisions over the coming years of fragile consolidation.
facets: The council's genuine knowledge of provincial unrest is irreplaceable, yet their incentives to distort information upward are structurally embedded in their own survival calculations.
Any visible test of honesty that humiliates a councilor will trigger defensive coalition-building among the others, potentially accelerating the very conspiracy the prince fears.

The prince's reputation for either credulity or paranoia will harden within the first two major policy decisions, shaping which external factions (nobility, merchant houses, foreign envoys) attempt to bypass the council entirely.

angles.directive: Introduce a deliberate, high-stakes policy fiction—propose an obviously flawed invasion plan—and observe which councilors formally endorse it versus those who risk objection; use the pattern not to purge, but to restructure the council into adversarial red/blue teams that compete to expose each other's blind spots.

Appoint a foreign-born steward with no local power base to conduct a six-month review of all counsel given during the previous reign, cross-referencing advice with actual outcomes; the steward reports patterns of flattery privately, absorbing the initial resentment while the prince remains above the inquiry.

Publicly stake a significant treasury reserve on a course of action championed by the most suspected flatterer, while privately preparing for its failure; let the costly error play out fully so the entire court witnesses the consequences, sacrificing short-term resources to permanently shift the council's norms toward cautious honesty.

Identify the most influential flatterer and promote them to a newly created honorific post—'Keeper of the Prince's Doubts'—whose sole remit is to argue against every major proposal; the role's prestige co-opts the flatterer's social capital while structurally compelling them to voice dissent, gradually modeling that contradiction carries reward rather than risk.

threads: The prince fabricates an obviously doomed invasion plan and demands a recorded vote. He then splits the council into a Red Team of objectors and a Blue Team of endorsers, mandating that each team publicly critique the other’s proposals before any decree. The move bets that careerist councilors, now forced to compete in exposing flaws, will preemptively dismantle each other’s distortions, turning the threat of internal conspiracy into a self-policing engine of accurate intelligence.

The prince appoints a foreign steward with no local power base to audit all counsel from the prior reign against outcomes, reporting patterns of flattery privately so the prince remains above the inquiry. The steward absorbs the council’s resentment as an unallied outsider, preventing a unified defensive coalition. The bet is that knowledge of the audit fractures the council preemptively: each member, uncertain whose advice will be exposed, begins feeding the prince unvarnished intelligence to prove personal loyalty, improving decision quality before any purge.

The prince stakes the treasury on a massive vanity project championed by the most suspected flatterer, secretly preparing a liquidity buffer to absorb the failure. He allows the predictable collapse to play out in full court view, letting the ruinous cost of sycophancy become undeniable. The bet is that witnessing this deliberate sacrifice permanently recalibrates the council’s incentives: the survivors will now fear that flattery invites public destruction, making honest risk assessment their safest path to survival.

Prince selects the council’s chief flatterer—the social hub—for the invented title Keeper of the Prince’s Doubts, charged with compulsorily drafting a formal rebuttal to every major proposal, co-opting his prestige to institutionalize dissent. This structural role makes his standing dependent on performing contradiction, demonstrating to all advisors that loyalty is expressed through rigorous opposition, not agreement. The anticipated pivot: within months, other councilors, seeing the Keeper’s influence undiminished, will preemptively inject genuine provincial data into their briefings to avoid appearing less useful than a man who only criticizes.

judge.note: Strategy 3 assumes council accepts treasury loss; Strategy 1 risks Red/Blue collusion.

judge6.note: Strategies 1,3 assume behavioral pivots without accounting for backlash.

theme: holding a newly conquered HEREDITARY state whose old ruling bloodline still has popular sympathy
problem: As the new ruler of a hereditary duchy, the old duke died heirless but his popular sister lives in exile with foreign friends; you must decide how to treat the conquered duchy's local magnates now, knowing her return is likely but its timing and allied backing are uncertain.
facets: The exiled sister is the emotional focus of residual loyalty but has no army of her own—yet.
Local magnates are watching your first moves for signals: too harsh and they fund her return, too soft and they think you weak.
A neighboring rival publicly praises her claim to pressure your border.

angles.directive: Bind the magnates to you by immediately confirming all their ancient privileges and granting new lucrative crown offices, then propose a marriage between your son and the exiled sister to merge bloodlines and drain her independent appeal before a foreign army materializes.

Strike first by publicly branding the sister a traitor conspiring with foreign powers, demanding her extradition while simultaneously arresting the most capable local magnates on fabricated evidence of plotting, replacing them with your own trusted men before any resistance can coordinate.

Decree that the old duchy will be the capital of a new, larger administrative province you are creating, dissolving its traditional boundaries and identity; flood the local council with outsiders loyal only to you, making the sister's claim to an entity that no longer politically exists seem archaic and irrelevant.

Withdraw your main force to a defensible garrison town on the border, publicly apologizing for the costs of the conquest and offering a plebiscite on the restoration of the sister as a figurehead duchess under your protection, betting that the magnates will soon splinter into pro- and anti-restoration factions and exhaust themselves rivaling for her favor while you conserve strength.

threads: Confirm the magnates’ ancestral land patents and tax exemptions in the first week, while appointing their leading sons as royal chamberlains and mint-masters. Simultaneously, dispatch a formal embassy to propose a binding betrothal between your heir and the exiled sister, offering her a restored seat on the ducal council. This bets that her foreign backers will not finance an invasion for a woman who has already accepted co-sovereignty, freezing their casus belli before it ripens.

I publicly brand the exiled sister a foreign-backed traitor and demand her extradition, forcing the neighboring rival to either surrender her—shattering her legitimacy—or refuse and reveal collusion. Simultaneously, I arrest the three most capable magnates on fabricated evidence of sedition and replace them with loyal castellans before their networks can react. The bet: decapitating local coordination turns her eventual return into a powerless symbol that the rival cannot openly support without accepting a war on my prepared ground.

I dissolve the old duchy by imperial decree, annexing its territory into a newly created macro-province governed from our ancestral seat and packing its council with bureaucrats imported from our core lands. This turns the sister into a claimant for a jurisdiction that legally no longer exists, forcing her foreign backers to frame intervention as crude land-grab rather than restoration. I am betting the local magnates, stripped of their traditional assembly and seeing their regional identity erased, will splinter between those who seek patronage in the new order and those who wait hopelessly for a ghost.

I withdraw the main force to the fortified border garrison at Thornhaven, issuing a public apology for the conquest’s cost and offering an immediate plebiscite to restore the sister as figurehead duchess under my suzerainty. This abrupt concession fractures the magnates: those enriched by the old duke’s fall will scramble to secure my protection against her return, while her loyalists split into rival cliques competing to be her indispensable champion—dissipating their combined strength in internal proxy warfare. As they exhaust themselves, my intact army deters any neighboring power from turning her claim into an invasion.

judge.note: Strategy 3 assumes magnate fracture; Strategy 4 assumes plebiscite credibility.

judge6.note: Strategy 3 (Transform/Reframe) borders on baroque; legal dissolution mechanics unclear.

theme: a prince raised by his own CRIME or betrayal, now consolidating an illegitimate seizure
problem: Having strangled the old duke and taken his state, a prince discovers that the duke's eight-year-old daughter has been spirited away to the court of a rival margrave who could use her claim to unite a coalition against him. He must now choose how to neutralize this threat before the next campaigning season, knowing the margrave is ambitious, his own captains are uneasy, and the people still whisper of the old duke's justice.
facets: The margrave’s ambition: he is a cautious but opportunistic player who will act only if he sees a high chance of success and enough allied support.
The mood of the people: they still remember the old duke’s fair rule and might rally to the daughter’s banner if she appears with a foreign army.
The loyalty of the prince’s own captains: they followed him for plunder but fear divine retribution for child-murder; their allegiance is not yet cemented in blood.

angles.directive: Send a trusted assassin to infiltrate the margrave's court and poison the girl, framing a rival faction to throw suspicion off himself, risking exposure that could unite all against him.

Open secret negotiations with the margrave, offering trade concessions and a non-aggression pact, while spreading rumors that the girl is a bastard with no true claim, buying time to consolidate his army and marry a foreign princess for a counter-alliance.

Publicly offer to adopt the girl as his own heir, promising a marriage to his nephew, thus merging bloodlines and giving the margrave a stake in his regime, but requiring intricate diplomacy to convince the girl and the margrave of his sincerity.

Launch a propaganda campaign painting the old duke as a tyrant who abused his daughter, and reveal (or fabricate) evidence that the margrave plans to use her as a puppet to enslave the kingdom, so that the people see her rescue as a foreign invasion rather than liberation.

threads: Send the prince’s physician, carrying a slow poison disguised as a remedy for the girl’s melancholy, to infiltrate the margrave’s court as a defector. He will dose her gradually until she sickens and dies, the death appearing natural. The margrave, suddenly bereft of a viable claimant on his own grounds, will see only danger: he will purge his inner circle rather than march, buying the prince a full campaigning season of inertia.

He opens secret negotiations offering trade concessions and a non-aggression pact, while spreading rumors that the girl is a bastard. This forces the margrave into a waiting game: he will not commit his forces until the taint of illegitimacy settles, for fear his allies defect over a disputed claim. With that pause, the prince weds a foreign princess, using her dowry to lock his captains and forge a counter-alliance that neutralizes the girl’s claim permanently.

The prince publicly adopts the murdered duke’s eight-year-old daughter as his sole heir and announces her betrothal to his nephew, merging the bloodlines and extinguishing her independent claim. He sends a high-ranking embassy to the margrave proposing a regency council for the girl’s minority, loaded with trade privileges and a son as a hostage to school at the margrave’s court, to bind him as a stakeholder. The move banks on the margrave’s ambition recognizing that a guaranteed role as guardian to the future consort yields more security than gambling on a coalition war he might not lead.

The prince dispathes agents to seed the countryside with forged letters and bribed witnesses, painting the old duke as a sadist who brutalized his daughter and the margrave as a would-be puppet-master bent on turning the kingdom into a client state. He ensures these stories reach every market town and temple before spring, so that when the margrave marches, the peasantry views the girl not as a rightful heir but as a broken tool of foreign conquest. The entire bet is that this preemptive narrative will paralyze the margrave’s recruitment among disaffected nobles, who will recoil from backing a campaign so easily framed as an invasion rather than a restoration.

judge.note: Strategy 3 assumes margrave accepts hostage; Strategy 4 assumes narrative penetrates elite.

judge6.note: Strategy 1 is assassination (illegal). Strategy 4 relies on propaganda working perfectly.

theme: FORTUNE vs CAUTION: whether to seize a sudden, fickle opportunity to expand
problem: A prince learns that a wealthy, strategically located city-state has rebelled against its master, who is temporarily paralyzed by a succession crisis; the city’s gates are open to any power that will guarantee its liberty. Seizing it now could provide a springboard for regional dominance, but the old master may quickly recover, neighboring rivals might unite against a sudden upstart, and the prince’s own resources are not yet fully mobilized.
facets: The speed and likelihood of the old master’s recovery from internal strife.
The shifting web of alliances and resentments among neighboring states, each watching the city hungrily.
The readiness and loyalty of the prince’s own military—citizen soldiers or mercenaries—and the risk of overextension.

angles.directive: Storm the city immediately, crush any lingering loyalists to the old master, and fortify it beyond easy recapture; then, if feasible, deliver a decisive blow to the reeling master to remove the threat permanently before rivals can coalesce.

Declare strict neutrality and let the city fall to the most aggressive neighbor; observe from a distance as that neighbor strains its forces and provokes a coalition against itself, then strike when the opportune moment shifts and the exhausted powers beg for your intervention.

Send envoys and secret subsidies to the city’s rebel leaders, promising protection and a generous charter that preserves their internal autonomy; gradually bind them through trade, intermarriage, and military coordination until the city becomes a willing satellite, avoiding overt conquest.

Proclaim a new ‘League of Free Cities’ and invite the rebellious city—and any other small powers—to join a mutual defense pact under your patronage, transforming the annexation into a collective liberation movement that reorders the regional balance and isolates the old master without making you the sole target of vengeance.

threads: Seize the city at dawn with the prince’s fastest household cavalry, publicly execute the old master’s appointed governor and his garrison commanders, and immediately issue a charter confirming the city’s liberties under a newly installed military prefect drawn from a local merchant faction. This decapitation strike bets that the old master, already paralyzed, will recoil into a defensive crouch rather than counterattack, buying the prince a six-month window to bring up his full levy and refortify the walls before neighboring rivals overcome their mutual suspicion to coordinate a response.

The prince will issue a formal proclamation of neutrality, refusing all entreaties to intervene, and quietly signal acceptance of the most aggressive neighbor’s occupation of the city. This move bets that the occupying power, burdened by the city’s defense and its own ambition, will immediately alarm its rivals into forming a punitive coalition. When that coalition grinds the occupier down and its members, drained and fearful of a vacuum, plead for the prince’s stabilizing hand, he will march in not as a conqueror but as a requested arbiter, securing the city at minimal cost.

Disguised envoys offer rebel leaders secret gold and a charter guaranteeing self-rule, forging defense pacts and exclusive trade that bind the city’s fate to the prince’s protection. The bet: the restored old master avoids besieging a city under the prince’s umbrella, knowing attack means full military retaliation and choked trade routes. Neighbors, seeing a fait accompli of interwoven trade rather than a land grab, will prefer accommodation to disruption of subsidized goods, forestalling any anti-prince coalition.

The prince proclaims a ‘League of Free Cities,’ extending an immediate mutual defense pact to the rebellious city and any other small polity seeking autonomy, with the prince’s own forces serving as the pact’s armed guarantor under a treaty that explicitly preserves each member’s internal liberty. This reframes the seizure as a collective liberation, not a unilateral annexation, forcing the old master to confront a coalition rather than a single usurper. The move banks on the old master’s recovery being paralyzed by the threat that any punitive strike against one member will trigger a cascade of defections from his remaining vassals into the League’s protective umbrella.

judge.note: Strategy 4 assumes vassal defection cascade; plausible but speculative.

judge6.note: Strategies 2 and 4 both exploit rival fragmentation; overlap in logic.

theme: a prince raised by his own CRIME or betrayal, now consolidating an illegitimate seizure
problem: A prince has seized a strategically vital city by bribing and murdering its garrison commander, but the dead commander's vengeful brother has rallied loyalist remnants and now threatens to open the gates to a rival power unless the prince can secure the city's obedience before the next campaigning season. The prince must decide how to neutralize this immediate threat while ensuring the city does not become a permanent drain or a staging ground for future insurrection.
facets: The city's merchant guilds are divided: some profited from the betrayal, others fear the instability will ruin trade.
The rival power is watching but has not yet committed troops, waiting to see if the brother's revolt gains momentum.
The prince's own army is ostentatiously loyal but contains officers who secretly admire the old garrison commander's honor and may waver.

angles.directive: Stage a public trial and execution of the brother for treason, using captured documents to 'prove' he conspired with the rival power, then purge any suspected sympathizers to decapitate the resistance in one stroke.
Offer the brother a high military office and marry your daughter to his son, transforming the vengeful heir into a co-ruler of the city whose fortunes now rise or fall with your own dynasty.

Announce the city will become a free republic under your protection, abolishing the old garrison system entirely and letting the merchant guilds elect their own council, thereby draining the brother's loyalist claim of all meaning.

Withdraw your main force to a defensible perimeter deeper in your territory, publicly apologizing for the seizure and paying weregild to the commander's family, absorbing the dishonor now so the rival power overextends by occupying a resentful city that will bleed them white.

threads: The prince stages a public treason trial and execution of the brother, using forged documents to implicate the rival power, then purges the garrison and merchant elite of suspected sympathizers in one night. This forces the rival to either disown their agent—losing credibility and future covert leverage—or launch a premature attack the prince’s loyal army can repel, isolating them politically. The shock and foreign-treason narrative unite the divided merchant guilds behind the prince, financing his fortifications and securing obedience for the campaign.

I will summon the vengeful brother and publicly invest him as the city’s hereditary military governor while simultaneously betrothing my daughter to his son, dissolving his vendetta by making the son my dynastic heir to the province. This mechanically converts the loyalist remnants into a vested garrison, as officers who admired the dead commander now guard a regime their fallen leader’s bloodline co-owns, not one they must overthrow. The move bets that the rival power, observing a legitimate succession compact instead of a rebellion, will refuse to commit troops, calculating that attacking a consolidated family fortress would unite the city against them and trap them in a costly siege without local allies.

The prince will formally dissolve the garrison and charter the city as a sovereign merchant republic under his military protectorate, immediately convening an election among the guilds for a ruling council whose first act will be to ratify a perpetual trade compact with his realm. This reframes the brother’s call to arms as an assault on the guilds’ own newfound liberty rather than a defense of the old order, making his loyalist remnants a threat to every merchant’s purse. The bet is that the profit-minded guilds will fund and raise their own militia to crush the brother themselves, sparing the prince’s army from a politically corrosive confrontation while shifting the cost of pacification onto the city.

I order our army to withdraw to the Red River fortifications and dispatch a herald with a public apology and a chest of silver weregild to the brother, absorbing the dishonor now. This voids our garrison commander’s gains but recasts the brother as a mere factionalist if he still admits the rival, splitting the loyalists. The rival power will then occupy a city of resentful guildsmen and unpaid veterans, forcing them to bleed coin and manpower on pacification while their officers grow tempted to extort the merchants, turning the city into an insurgency that unravels their coalition.

judge.note: Strategy 4 assumes rival miscalculates; others assume rational responses.

judge6.note: Strategy 1 relies on forged documents; Strategy 4 assumes rival will occupy despite withdrawal signal.

theme: SERVANT CORRUPTION (a supplier or cook skimming) where exposing it fractures the household
problem: The head cook, uncle to a rising concubine, has been skimming silver from the kitchen accounts in league with a grain supplier, and he holds a secret about your past that could ruin you. Exposing his theft could either consolidate your stewardship or shatter the household into warring factions, depending on whether the master believes you, the concubine retaliates, and the servants choose sides.
facets: The cook’s gossip network among servants can shape reputations and turn the household against you before any formal accusation takes hold.
The concubine’s current favor makes her a powerful protector; if her star falls first, the cook would be exposed with far less risk.
The cook knows of a past indiscretion you thought was buried—any open move against him could trigger mutually assured destruction.

angles.directive: Assemble irrefutable evidence of the cook’s skimming and present it to the master in a single stroke, while planting a preemptive rumor that the cook’s secrets are merely a desperate criminal’s lies, aiming to have him expelled before he can retaliate with your own secret.

Secretly document every transaction but take no action now; wait for the concubine’s influence to wane or for the cook to offend a more powerful rival, then release the evidence through a neutral servant to trigger an organic downfall that keeps your hands clean.

Reveal to the cook privately that you know his scheme, but instead of threatening him, offer to embed his skimming into a larger, controlled fraud that enriches both of you, turning him into a double agent who feeds you information on the concubine and other rivals in exchange for protection.

Propose a sweeping household reform to the master, centralizing all provisioning through a single approved merchant—your own trusted ally—thereby eliminating the cook’s purchasing role entirely while framing it as a cost-saving modernization, neutering his scheme without direct accusation.

threads: I lay before the master the grain merchant’s signed testimony and the cook’s doctored weekly accounts, while simultaneously planting among the servants that a cornered embezzler always spews false accusations. The master, incensed by the documented loss, will order immediate banishment before the concubine can intercede, and the pre-spread rumor turns the cook’s inevitable scream into the expected lie of a trapped rat. The entire play hinges on the fact that cold numbers, once seen, make any verbal defense sound like a conspirator’s plea.

I will quietly duplicate the kitchen ledgers and grain receipts, assembling a complete financial record that proves the cook’s embezzlement, then bury the dossier until the concubine’s favor inevitably cools or the cook overreaches against a senior house servant. My move is to wait for that shift and then slip the documents to a disaffected scullery maid with no ties to either faction, letting her “discover” the theft and bring it to the steward’s deputy. I am betting that by the time the evidence surfaces, the master will already be distancing himself from the concubine, and the cook’s own gossip network will fracture as servants realize he has been stealing from the household budget that pays their wages, turning their resentment against him and leaving my hands surgically clean.

I pull the cook aside with a forged grain ledger and, instead of threats, propose we inflate his existing skim into a joint fund siphoned from the concubine’s own household purse, laundering the excess through padded supplier invoices that I alone approve. In exchange, he feeds me her private complaints and impending alliances, positioning him as my planted ear. The gamble is that his nervous double-dealing will leak to her, provoking a panicked, public denouncement of me—which I will recast as proof she is mounting a factional coup against the master’s steward.

I will convince the master to consolidate all kitchen procurement under a single vetted merchant—my own loyal ally—framing the overhaul as an austerity measure to cut waste, which dissolves the cook’s financial function overnight without a single accusation. The reform bypasses his skimming entirely: he no longer touches silver, so his secret ledger with the grain supplier becomes irrelevant. This bets that the cook, trapped between swallowing the new structure and protesting it, will either accept demotion quietly or lash out baselessly, revealing himself to the master as obstructive rather than trustworthy.

judge.note: Strategies 1&3 assume master credulity; 2&4 underestimate concubine agency.

judge6.note: Strategies 1, 3 collapse under scrutiny; 2, 4 plausible but fragile.

theme: a neglected concubine selling household INTELLIGENCE to rival houses for favor and silver
problem: A neglected concubine has been selling household intelligence to a rival merchant for silver and favor; she now discovers that the rival intends to use her secrets to ruin her own household, while her suspicious master may be laying a trap. Her next move—before either side acts—will determine whether she survives, prospers, or is destroyed.
facets: The rival's intent to seize the household’s assets turns her from informer into a disposable tool, making continued cooperation a death sentence if the trap springs.
The master’s possible trap and the household’s existing hierarchy mean that confession could be rewarded as loyalty or punished as weakness, depending on how it’s presented.
The other wives and concubines would eagerly exploit any sign of her treachery to eliminate a rival, so any move that reveals her guilt must account for their opportunism.

angles.directive: Confront the rival openly, demanding a large payment to remain silent and threatening to expose his scheme to the master, counting on his fear of mutual destruction to make him withdraw.
Slip away in secret with the silver and a trusted ally, abandoning both households and letting the rival’s plot and the master’s trap unfold without her presence to be blamed.

Approach the master or the first wife, confess everything but reframe the spying as a deliberate ploy to feed false intelligence, offering to become a double agent who will help ruin the rival, thus integrating her betrayal into a household counter-strategy.

Leak the rival’s plot to a neutral magistrate as a commercial conspiracy, positioning herself as a coerced informer seeking to expose fraud, and reframe the entire affair as a public-interest case where her past leaks become evidence of forced compliance.

threads: She will confront the rival in his own counting room with a sealed ledger detailing every transaction, demanding a lump-sum buyout equivalent to two years’ profit and a signed withdrawal from the household’s debt contracts—payment in hand before she leaves. She bets that his immediate fear of exposure to the master, whose retaliatory arson he has witnessed before, will compel him to pay and pivot his scheme elsewhere rather than risk mutually assured destruction. The downstream consequence she is counting on is that the rival, once bought off, will quietly sever her from his network, leaving no loose thread for the master’s trap to snag.

She will slip away before dawn with the silver and the elderly steward whose loyalty she quietly bought months ago by sharing small portions of her illicit earnings, leaving behind a forged letter in the rival’s hand that “accidentally” surfaces in the master’s study, framing the rival for the entire conspiracy. This bets that both the rival and the master will fixate on each other as the sole enemy once they discover the planted evidence and her disappearance, exhausting their fury in mutual destruction while her absence reads as terrified flight from the rival’s coercion rather than guilt.

She approaches the master directly, confessing every transaction but recasting it as a long deception: she fed the rival tailored falsehoods to map his intentions, and now offers to deliver the final bait—a fabricated vulnerability in the household’s accounts—that will lure him into a ruinous premature strike. The move bets that the master’s hunger to destroy the rival will override his fury, converting her from target to weapon. Once the rival acts on her next report and his assets are seized mid-scheme, her indispensability to that victory inoculates her against the other wives, who cannot denounce her without unpicking the master’s own triumph.

She slips a sworn affidavit to the district magistrate, detailing the rival’s plan to ruin her household through fraudulent commercial maneuvers, and claims she acted under duress as a forced informant. She reframes all her past intelligence leaks as compelled compliance, now voluntarily disclosed to expose the conspiracy. She is betting that the magistrate will immediately open a formal inquiry, freezing the rival’s assets and compelling her master to publicly embrace her as a reluctant whistleblower, since any retaliation would be seen as obstruction of a commercial fraud investigation.

judge.note: Strategies 3&4 assume unrealistic master/magistrate cooperation chains.

judge6.note: Strategy 4 relies on magistrate intervention; assumes legal recourse exists and functions.

theme: punishing a wealthy concubine when doing so risks her SUICIDE, public SCANDAL, and loss of her fortune
problem: Ximen Qing must discipline his wealthy, sensitive concubine Li Ping'er for openly insulting the principal wife, Moon Lady; harsh punishment risks Li Ping'er's suicide, a ruinous public scandal, and the loss of the fortune that sustains the household. A lenient response would shatter his authority and embolden rivals like Pan Jinlian, so he needs a move that anticipates how each faction will counter-move over the next few weeks.
facets: Li Ping'er's emotional fragility and realistic suicide risk
Her immense dowry and the household's financial dependence on it
The volatile coalition of rival wives who will exploit any outcome

angles.directive: Punish her openly to reassert dominance, but secretly place a trusted maid as her shadow to prevent suicide, while leaking a sanitized version to the gossip network that frames it as a minor domestic correction—contain the scandal by force of narrative control.

Postpone judgment publicly by invoking the need for astrological divination; use the delay to let other crises surface, hoping that Pan Jinlian's attention shifts or Li Ping'er falls ill, making the original insult irrelevant by a changed landscape.

Privately offer Li Ping'er a public elevation—host a reconciliation banquet in her honor—while extracting a promise that she will voluntarily transfer a portion of her private jewelry to Moon Lady’s coffers, turning punishment into a show of magnanimity and securing material appeasement.

Reframe the insult as a pre-scripted test of household loyalty that Li Ping'er performed at your secret command; expose the accusers as having failed the test, punish them for their haste, and reward Li Ping'er—converting the entire episode into a trap that strengthens your hand.

threads: Ximen Qing will publicly flog Li Ping’er in the inner courtyard to reassert dominance, but beforehand he secretly installs his most loyal maid, Chunmei, as her constant shadow with explicit instructions to intercept any self-harm attempt. Simultaneously, he leaks a sanitized account to the household gossip network describing the flogging as a token ritual correction for a minor breach of etiquette, not a humiliation. The bet is that Pan Jinlian, expecting a scandal she can weaponize, will overreach by spreading exaggerated tales of cruelty, which Chunmei’s counter-narrative will discredit as jealous fabrication, neutralizing her faction before it coheres.

Ximen Qing publicly postpones punishment by insisting that proper discipline requires casting Li Ping'er’s astrological chart, summoning a diviner for a consultation he deliberately schedules ten days out. The interlude transforms the household’s focus: Li Ping'er, suspended in dread, begins to exhibit nervous exhaustion, while Pan Jinlian’s impatient scheming overreaches into visible cruelty. He is betting that Li Ping'er’s impending collapse will recast her not as an insolent wife but as a fragile invalid whose dowry and survival the household cannot risk, thereby dissolving the original insult in a tide of pragmatic concern.

Ximen Qing privately offers Li Ping’er a public reconciliation banquet in her honor, extracting her promise to voluntarily gift a portion of her jewelry to Moon Lady’s coffers. At the banquet, Moon Lady’s acceptance of the jewels publicly locks her into endorsing Li Ping’er’s contrition, transforming the insult into a staged alliance. The move forces Pan Jinlian to either celebrate the harmony or expose her own faction’s malice, isolating her and consolidating Ximen Qing’s control over the household’s emotional and financial levers.

Ximen Qing summons the entire household and announces that Li Ping’er’s insult was a loyalty probe he secretly ordered, then immediately demotes Pan Jinlian and the other accusers for their eagerness to condemn without first seeking his will, while rewarding Li Ping’er with a private token of trust. This publicly recasts the episode as proof that Li Ping’er is his most obedient instrument and that her fortune now anchors a regime that punishes disloyalty, decisively stripping the rival coalition of the moral high ground. The move bets that Pan Jinlian, publicly humiliated and stripped of credibility, will be forced into a desperate scramble to demonstrate loyalty on his terms, buying weeks of paralysis in which her faction dares not challenge Li Ping’er for fear of walking into another trap.

judge.note: Strategy 4 risks backfiring if household doubts the 'loyalty probe' framing.

judge6.note: Strategies 1&4 assume rival overreach; 2&3 assume psychological collapse. Thin empirical grounding.

theme: exploiting a sudden battlefield ADVANTAGE to force a hard PEACE now vs pressing on for total victory
problem: You are an Athenian general who has just shattered the Spartan fleet at Naxos, leaving the Spartan commander dead and his ships destroyed; you can now credibly demand a favorable peace that isolates Sparta from its allies, or you can press on to attack the Peloponnesian coast and dismantle Sparta’s entire naval capacity forever.
facets: Athens' treasury is severely depleted after years of war, and the Assembly may not fund another season of campaigning unless you bring back plunder or a treaty.
Sparta's allied Corinthian fleet is still intact at its harbor in Lechaion, and any delay allows them to regroup and join with other Peloponnesian forces.
The Athenian fleet's crews are exhausted and far from home, and a single storm or reverse could turn a triumphant expedition into a catastrophe with no rescue.

angles.directive: Exploit the shock and disorganization of the enemy to strike the Corinthian fleet at Lechaion immediately, destroying the last major Peloponnesian naval power in a single decisive blow before they can prepare a defense.

Send envoys to Corinth and other Spartan allies now, offering a separate peace that recognizes their autonomy and grants them favorable trade terms, peeling them away from Sparta and presenting Athens as the leader of a new maritime league rather than a conqueror.

Use the victory to proclaim a pan-Hellenic naval alliance against the Persian threat, inviting even Sparta to join under Athenian leadership, thereby reframing the conflict from a war between Greek states into a common defense of Hellas and making those who refuse seem unpatriotic.

Withdraw the fleet to Athens with the Spartan prisoners and spoils, let the Assembly claim the glory and negotiate from a position of demonstrated strength, while preserving the fleet as a deterrent, so that the costs and risks of any further offensive fall on the city’s democratic decision rather than your own command.

threads: Sail the intact Athenian battle line immediately to Lechaion under cover of darkness, launching a concentrated ramming and boarding assault at dawn with fire ships in the van to burn the Corinthian triremes still beached or moored, giving them no time to form a defensive line. This eliminates the last Peloponnesian fleet in a single stroke and turns their hulls and naval stores into the plunder that secures Assembly funding, rendering further campaigning unnecessary.

Dispatch Callias and a handpicked diplomatic corps to Lechaion at first light with a ratified decree offering Corinth full autonomy, exemption from tribute for five years, and privileged access to the Piraeus grain route in exchange for immediate neutrality and naval disarmament. Simultaneously, send fast triremes to Epidaurus, Troezen, and Hermione carrying identical terms, transforming Sparta’s remaining coastal allies into neutral buffer states under Athenian economic hegemony before the Spartan gerousia can convene.

Dispatch Athenian heralds to every Greek polis—including Sparta—to formally propose a permanent Pan-Hellenic naval alliance against Persia, with a unified treasury funded by proportional contributions from all members and an Athenian-appointed navarch holding operational command over the combined fleet. Simultaneously, present the decree to the Athenian Assembly as the fulfillment of the victory at Naxos, framing any refusal—especially Sparta’s—as a betrayal of Hellenic freedom that would forfeit the right to call upon Greek aid against any future barbarian invasion.

Withdraw the fleet and prisoners directly to Piraeus, then immediately request the Assembly convene an ekklesia where you present the spoils and captives as evidence of victory while formally ceding the decision on further operations to a public vote, thereby transferring the financial and strategic risk of an extended campaign onto the city’s democratic apparatus and off your command.

judge.note: Strategy 3 fantasizes Pan-Hellenic unity; Strategy 4 evades rather than decides.

judge6.note: Strategy 3 overreaches; Strategy 4 abdicates. Foresight shallow.

theme: whether a dominant assembly should ANNIHILATE or spare a REVOLTED SUBJECT-CITY as a warning to other restless allies
problem: The Athenian Assembly must decide the punishment of Mytilene, a crucial naval ally that has just been recaptured after a dangerous revolt; the choice will signal to every other subject-city whether rebellion invites annihilation or a more measured response.
facets: The empire's other subject-cities are watching, and excessive cruelty may harden resistance in future revolts, while clemency may be seen as weakness.
The Mytilenean oligarchs who instigated the revolt have already been surrendered; the common people are largely ambivalent, offering an opportunity to split the population.
Athenian naval dominance hinges on tribute and alliance; destroying Mytilene removes a potential naval base, but keeping it intact requires garrison costs and risk of re-revolt.

angles.directive: Execute all adult males, enslave the women and children, and demolish the city’s fortifications to serve as a stark deterrent.
Transport the captive population to Athens and hold them indefinitely as hostages, leaving a small garrison to administer the city while deferring any final sentence.
Spare the population, execute only the ringleaders, install a democratic regime, and station a permanent garrison to bind Mytilene as a loyal tribute-paying ally.

Pardon the entire city and announce a new imperial charter offering equal trade rights and citizenship pathways to all allies who remain loyal, transforming the empire into a league of mutual benefit.

threads: To split the population is to leave the empire's spine exposed; instead, execute every Mytilenean male of arms-bearing age, enslave the rest, and raze the city’s walls and docks to the waterline so that the empty site becomes a navigational warning rather than a port.

The Athenian fleet will immediately embark the entire Mytilenean demos—excluding the surrendered ringleaders—and transport them to Athens for indefinite custodial detention as living collateral against the island’s future compliance. A skeleton garrison of two hundred hoplites will remain to administer the city and extract tribute, while the Assembly formally suspends judgment, converting the population into a permanent, inert bargaining chip rather than a corpse or a free ally.

Identify and summarily execute the 30 surrendered oligarchic ringleaders, then immediately convene a Mytilenean assembly to ratify a democratic charter and elect a pro-Athenian council under the supervision of an Athenian garrison commander.  
Station a permanent garrison of 500 hoplites and a trireme squadron in the harbor, funded by a fixed annual tribute reassessed at 15 talents, thereby converting Mytilene from a rebellious naval ally into a securely garrisoned democratic tributary.

The Assembly immediately issues a universal pardon to Mytilene and proclaims a new Imperial Charter granting full trade reciprocity and a five-year military service path to Athenian citizenship for any allied city that pays its tribute without interruption. The charter is funded by converting a fixed percentage of tribute into a league-managed naval infrastructure fund, with voting seats on its allocation board allocated proportionally to contribution levels, thereby transforming the coercive empire into an opt-in mutual defense and commercial league.

judge.note: Strategy 4 fantasizes opt-in empire; others lack reaction chains.

judge6.note: Strategy 4 unrealistic; Strategy 2 creates unsustainable logistics.

theme: whether a dominant assembly should ANNIHILATE or spare a REVOLTED SUBJECT-CITY as a warning to other restless allies
problem: Following the suppression of Mytilene’s revolt, the Athenian Assembly must determine the city's fate to deter other tribute-paying allies from rebellion, knowing that the fleet holds the city but allied resentment is already hardening across the empire.
facets: The demos and the rowers demand swift, spectacular revenge after a costly siege, threatening domestic stability if denied.
Other restive allies are watching closely; excessive cruelty may provoke desperate resistance rather than obedience.
Mytilene’s harbor and fleet represented a major asset to the league, whose destruction or transformation carries long-term economic and strategic consequences.

angles.directive: Annihilate Mytilene: execute all adult males, enslave women and children, raze its walls, and leave the site desolate as an unforgettable warning to all subjects.

Spare the populace, try and execute only the ringleaders, impose a democratic constitution and a permanent garrison, and integrate Mytilene’s fleet into the League with favored terms to bind its interests to Athens.
Summon a tribunal of allied cities to judge Mytilene’s guilt and pronounce sentence, sharing responsibility and making the League complicit in whatever follows.

Dissolve Mytilene as an independent polis and refound it as an Athenian cleruchy, expropriating land for Athenian settlers and erasing the rebellious identity by making it a permanent extension of Attica.

threads: Annihilate every Mytilenean male of arms-bearing age, transport the women and children to the Athenian slave markets to offset the siege’s cost, demolish the harbor fortifications, and scuttle their triremes so that the empty anchorage becomes a permanent visual scar on the Aegean trade routes.

We will disembark the ringleaders identified by the returning exiles, try them on the spot, and execute them publicly, while the demos is immediately convened to ratify a new democratic constitution under the protection of a permanent Athenian garrison. Simultaneously, we will absorb the Mytilenean triremes into the League fleet but grant their crews a five-year exemption from tribute payments, converting their naval capacity into a privileged instrument of Athenian power that makes defection costlier than loyalty.

To execute this, Athens convenes a formal judicial panel composed of one voting delegate from each tributary ally, tasking them with reviewing the evidence of Mytilene’s breach of treaty and issuing a binding collective sentence that the Athenian fleet then mechanically enforces.

The Assembly should decree the abolition of Mytilene as a corporate entity and carve its territory into 3,000 hereditary land allotments assigned by lot to Athenian cleruchs, who will garrison the harbor and operate its trireme sheds as a permanent naval station under an Athenian governor. This surgically replaces a restive tributary with a piece of Attica that pays no tribute but generates fleet capacity and a landed surveillance class, turning a liability into an imperial organ.

judge.note: Strategies 1,2,4 lack realistic downstream modeling; Strategy 3 underspecified.

judge6.note: Strategy 3 delegates accountability; strategies 1&4 blur (both eliminate corporate entity).

theme: accepting a charismatic envoy's promise of ALLIANCE AND REINFORCEMENT that may be a bluff or a trap
problem: As the democratic leader of Corcyra holding the city after a vicious civil war, you receive a charismatic exiled oligarchic envoy promising that if you allow the oligarchs to return peacefully and share power, they will bring a Corinthian fleet already at sea to defend against an imminent Athenian punitive expedition—this salvation might be genuine, or a trap to gain entry and slaughter your faction.
facets: The envoy's eloquence and apparent sincerity make the promise compelling but impossible to verify before the gates are opened.
Your exhausted city lacks the strength to fight both the returning oligarchs and an Athenian assault, making outside reinforcement desperately needed.
The oligarchic faction has a known history of oath-breaking and brutal reprisals, yet their fear of Athenian destruction might now force genuine cooperation.

angles.directive: Arrest the envoy and execute him publicly as a spy, using his death to harden your faction’s resolve to fight alone and deter any internal conspiracy.
Keep the envoy in comfortable but closely watched seclusion, stalling for time while secretly dispatching your own fast ship to scout for the Corinthian fleet before giving any answer.

Accept the offer but insist the oligarchs first prove their good faith by sending their promised fleet captains ashore unarmed to help repair the city’s fortifications, merging their fate with yours under your supervision.

Secretly inform the approaching Athenians of the envoy’s proposal, offering to act as a double agent; this transforms the negotiation into a means to bait the oligarchs and Corinth into a premature engagement on your terms.

threads: Seize the envoy mid-address, charge him with espionage under wartime decree, and execute him publicly at the main gate within the hour. His displayed corpse will cauterize all internal wavering, forcing every citizen into irreversible commitment and preempting any oligarchic fifth column from coordinating with the fleet.

I will place the envoy in the inner guest chambers under constant armed watch, framing it as honored protection while explaining that the Assembly must deliberate; simultaneously, I will hand-pick a crew for the *Aetos*, our fastest trireme, with sealed orders to sail north immediately, scout the approaches to Corcyra, and return with a confirmed sighting—or absence—of the Corinthian fleet before any gates are unbarred.

"I condition the oligarchs’ return on the immediate, unarmed disembarkation of their Corinthian fleet captains to join work gangs repairing the Long Walls under direct loyalist militia supervision, merging their personal survival with the city’s physical defenses. This move transforms their naval expertise into a directly controlled defensive asset while the unarmed vulnerability functions as a non-negotiable litmus test—compliance yields both reinforcement and hostages, refusal exposes the trap without ever opening a gate."

Dispatch a swift, unmarked skiff under cover of night to the Athenian fleet with a sealed offer to become their intelligence asset, feeding them the exact timing and signal for the oligarchs’ re-entry so the Athenians can intercept the Corinthian squadron at the harbor mouth. This converts the negotiation from a binary open-or-shut decision into a controlled anvil-and-hammer operation, where the city gates open only to spring a predetermined Athenian trap on both the returning oligarchs and their Corinthian protectors.

judge.note: Strategy 4 assumes Athenian cooperation without plausible mechanism; risks catastrophic miscalculation.

judge6.note: Strategy 4 is espionage betrayal; Strategy 3 hostage-taking dubious under law.

theme: whether a dominant assembly should ANNIHILATE or spare a REVOLTED SUBJECT-CITY as a warning to other restless allies
problem: The Athenian assembly must determine the fate of Mytilene, a subject-city that revolted, killed Athenian captives, and surrendered on promise of a fair hearing; now the assembly debates whether to annihilate the city as a warning to other restless allies or to uphold the general's pledge, knowing that leniency might embolden further revolts.
facets: The strategic logic of deterrence: a swift, brutal punishment could terrify other allies into submission, but destroying a major city costs tribute and manpower.
The binding force of the general's surrender terms: breaking the promise undermines Athenian honor and future negotiations, yet honouring it risks appearing weak.
The volatile mood of the democratic assembly, whipped by demagogues like Cleon, who frame clemency as sentimental folly that endangers the empire.

angles.directive: Vote to kill every adult male Mytilenean, enslave the women and children, and raze the city walls and harbour, leaving the site desolate as a permanent deterrent.

Spare the entire city unconditionally, reaffirm the surrender terms, restore the democratic faction, and reduce the annual tribute slightly to convert Mytilene into a loyal demonstration of Athenian magnanimity.

Settle 1,000 Athenian cleruchs on confiscated lands of the ringleaders, turning Mytilene into a military colony while leaving the rest of the population free but disarmed, thereby permanently altering its identity and preventing future rebellion.

Hand over the city to a pro-Athenian exile faction from Lesbos, empowering them to execute the oligarchic rebels and govern as a client regime under Athenian protection, so that the bloodshed is done by local hands and Athens avoids direct moral and political stain.

threads: The assembly executes every adult male Mytilenean, sells the women and children into slavery, and dismantles the city’s walls and harbour, leaving the site a depopulated ruin to function as a permanent, visible deterrent. This move converts the city itself into a negative monument—erasing its strategic utility and broadcast capacity while preserving the empire’s manpower pool through the enslaved survivors.

The assembly will issue an immediate decree annulling the prior death sentence, formally renewing the surrender pact, installing the returned Mytilenean democrats in power, and lowering the annual tribute from the original assessment by ten percent. This converts Mytilene from a crushed enemy into a permanently indebted client whose visible prosperity under Athenian mercy discredits rebellion as futile and strips future revolts of any moral cover.

The assembly executes the 1,000 identified ringleaders, confiscates their estates, and settles 1,000 Athenian cleruchs on that land as a permanently armed garrison-farming class, while disarming the remaining Mytileneans but leaving them free and tribute-paying. The Mytilenean fleet is seized and absorbed into the Athenian navy, the city walls are razed, and the cleruchy’s presence transforms the polis into a demilitarized agricultural dependency from which rebellion is physically impossible.

Recall the pro-Athenian Lesbian exiles, place the surrendered city under their legal jurisdiction, and have them publicly convict and execute the oligarchic rebels under Mytilenean law.  
Athens then formalizes their regime as a tribute-paying client state under garrison protection, transforming the retribution into a localized purge that leaves no Athenian hand directly bloodied.

judge.note: Strategy 2's 'visible prosperity' claim underspecified; Strategy 4's exile-regime stability unexamined.

judge6.note: Strategies 2 and 3 both co-opt via settlement; overlap muddies separation.

theme: a besieged city's choice to SURRENDER on terms, hold out for a rescue that may never come, or break out by force
problem: Cleonymus, the Spartan harmost besieged in the rebel city of Scione with dwindling supplies, must choose a course before the Athenian circumvallation starves his garrison and the promised Peloponnesian fleet arrives too late—or not at all.
